meta.title: The Philosophers' Index
meta.subtitle: Twenty-six centuries of thought, across every tradition — searchable.

glossary.heading: Fields of philosophy — what each discipline studies

terms.Aesthetics: Studies beauty, art, and taste. Asks what makes something beautiful or sublime, what art is and does, and why aesthetic experience matters.
terms.Epistemology: The theory of knowledge. Asks what knowledge is, what justifies belief, whether we can know anything at all, and how perception and reason give us access to the world.
terms.Ethics: The study of right action, moral value, and how to live. Covers both individual conduct and the foundations of moral judgment itself.
terms.Feminist: Examines how gender, power, and social structures shape knowledge, ethics, and politics. Argues mainstream philosophy has systematically excluded women's experience.
terms.Logic: The study of valid inference and formal reasoning. Underpins mathematics and all rigorous argument; ranges from Aristotle's syllogistics to modern mathematical logic.
terms.Metaphysics: Asks the most fundamental questions: what exists, what is real, what is time, what is causation, whether there is free will. The deepest layer of philosophical inquiry.
terms.Philosophy of language: Examines how words and sentences mean things, how language relates to thought, and what truth is. Central to both analytic philosophy and continental thought.
terms.Philosophy of mind: Asks what the mind is, how consciousness arises from the brain, and whether mental states are reducible to physical ones. Intersects with cognitive science and neuroscience.
terms.Philosophy of religion: Examines religious belief philosophically: arguments for and against God's existence, the nature of faith, the problem of evil, and religious experience.
terms.Philosophy of science: Investigates what science is, how it produces knowledge, what explanation and causation mean, and whether scientific theories describe reality or merely predict it.
terms.Phenomenology/Existentialism: Phenomenology describes structures of conscious experience; existentialism asks what it means to exist as a free being in a world without given meaning. Often intertwined.
terms.Political philosophy: Studies legitimacy, justice, authority, and rights. Asks what makes a government just, what citizens owe each other, and how power should be distributed.
terms.Pragmatism: Holds that the meaning and truth of an idea lie in its practical consequences. Treats inquiry as problem-solving and is skeptical of fixed foundations, favoring what works in experience.
terms.Social/Critical theory: Analyzes how social structures, ideology, and power reproduce inequality. Draws on Marx, Freud, and sociology to critique culture, institutions, and knowledge itself.

glossary.heading: Traditions — where each school of thought comes from

terms.African/Africana: Philosophical traditions rooted in African cultures and the African diaspora. Includes Ubuntu ethics, sage philosophy, anticolonial thought, and Africana pragmatism.
terms.Analytic: Dominant in Britain and North America from the early 20th century. Prizes precision, logical analysis of language, and engagement with science. Descends from Frege, Russell, and Moore.
terms.Ancient Greek/Roman: The founding tradition of Western philosophy, from the pre-Socratics through Plato and Aristotle to the Hellenistic schools (Stoics, Epicureans, Skeptics) and Roman inheritors.
terms.Buddhist: Philosophical thought arising from the Buddha's teachings, spanning Theravada, Madhyamaka, Yogacara, and Vajrayana schools. Engages deeply with mind, ethics, and the nature of self.
terms.Classical Chinese: Encompasses Confucianism, Daoism, Mohism, Legalism, and Neo-Confucianism. Centered on ethics, governance, and cosmology rather than formal logic or metaphysics of substance.
terms.Classical Indian: One of the world's richest philosophical traditions: six orthodox schools (Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Samkhya, Yoga, Mimamsa, Vedanta) plus Jain and other heterodox systems.
terms.Continental: European philosophy from Hegel onward, running through phenomenology, existentialism, structuralism, and poststructuralism. Values history, language, and the limits of reason.
terms.Feminist: Philosophical work centering gender as a category of analysis. Spans liberal, radical, socialist, poststructuralist, and care-ethics feminisms.
terms.Islamic: Philosophy written in the Islamic world, from the 9th century onward. Transmitted and transformed Greek thought; produced original metaphysics, epistemology, and mystical philosophy.
terms.Jewish: Philosophical engagement with Jewish texts and tradition, from medieval synthesis with Aristotle (Maimonides) to modern existentialist and ethical thought (Levinas, Rosenzweig).
terms.Latin American: Philosophy produced in or about Latin America. Grapples with colonial history, indigenous thought, identity, and liberation. Often explicitly political.
terms.Medieval European: Scholastic philosophy of Christian Europe, roughly 500–1500. Worked to reconcile Greek philosophy with Christian theology; developed logic and metaphysics considerably.
terms.Modern European (Empiricism): 17th–18th century British tradition holding that knowledge comes primarily from sensory experience. Key figures: Locke, Berkeley, Hume.
terms.Modern European (Idealism): German tradition, late 18th–early 19th century, arguing reality is mind-dependent. Runs from Kant through Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel.
terms.Modern European (Rationalism): 17th century Continental tradition holding that reason, not experience, is the primary source of knowledge. Key figures: Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz.
terms.Modern European: Broader early modern and Enlightenment thought not neatly rationalist or empiricist: Hobbes, Rousseau, Voltaire, Smith, and others.
terms.Pragmatist: American school holding that meaning and truth are determined by practical consequences. Founded by Peirce; developed by James, Dewey, and revived by Rorty and Brandom.

glossary.heading: Eras — how to read the historical periods

terms.Ancient (to 500 CE): Covers pre-Socratic cosmology, classical Greek philosophy, Hellenistic schools, and late antique Neoplatonism. Also includes classical Chinese, Indian, and Buddhist philosophy from the same centuries.
terms.Medieval (500–1500): Christian scholasticism in Europe, Islamic falsafa and kalam, Jewish philosophy, and mature Indian systems (Vedanta, Nyaya, Jain logic). Dominated by reconciling reason with revealed religion.
terms.Early modern (1500–1800): The Renaissance, Reformation, and Enlightenment. Rationalism, empiricism, and the scientific revolution reshape philosophy. Political philosophy becomes central (Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau).
terms.19th century: German idealism peaks and fractures into Marx, Kierkegaard, and Nietzsche. Utilitarianism, early feminism, and the first systematic social theory emerge. Philosophy begins specializing.
terms.Modern & contemporary: Analytic and continental traditions diverge. Logic, language, and mind dominate in the Anglophone world; phenomenology, critical theory, and poststructuralism in Europe. Non-Western traditions gain wider recognition.

meta.footer: Each entry links to its Wikipedia article. Popularity is average English Wikipedia views per day over a rolling 10-year window, normalized only for display. Field tags are inclusive — a thinker tagged in several fields appears under each. Dates for ancient and pre-modern figures are scholarly estimates (“c.” = circa, “fl.” = flourished). Built as a browsable reference; corrections welcome.

entries.name: Abelard, Peter
entries.desc: Scholastic logician; nominalism, ethics of intention.
entries.tldr: Medieval France's sharpest logician and one of its most dramatic figures. He argued that universals are just words, not real things. His ethics turned on intention: what makes an act wrong is inner consent to evil, not the deed itself. His catastrophic love affair with Héloïse and subsequent castration by her uncle made him a legend in his own time.

entries.name: Adorno, Theodor
entries.desc: Frankfurt School; negative dialectics, culture-industry critique.
entries.tldr: Co-author of Dialectic of Enlightenment, Adorno argued that the Enlightenment's drive to dominate nature through reason ended up dominating people too — producing the Holocaust and the culture industry in the same breath. His 'negative dialectics' refused tidy synthesis: thought should preserve what resists being subsumed. He believed easy writing falsified hard reality.

entries.name: Al-Farabi
entries.desc: “Second Teacher”; political philosophy, Aristotelian logic in Islam.
entries.tldr: The 'Second Teacher' (after Aristotle) transmitted Greek philosophy to the Islamic world and made it politically useful. He argued the ideal state is structured like the cosmos — a unified hierarchy with a philosopher-prophet at its head. His commentaries on Aristotle's logic set the terms for Islamic philosophy for centuries.

entries.name: Al-Ghazali
entries.desc: Critiqued the philosophers; revived Sufism within orthodoxy.
entries.tldr: The great critic of Islamic philosophy, Al-Ghazali wrote The Incoherence of the Philosophers to demolish Avicenna and Al-Farabi. He argued 20 of their positions were heretical. But he was no pure fideist — his Revival of the Religious Sciences is a sophisticated synthesis of Sufism and Islamic law that revitalized religious practice.

entries.name: Al-Kindi
entries.desc: First Arab Peripatetic; introduced Greek philosophy to Islam.
entries.tldr: The first philosopher writing in Arabic, Al-Kindi oversaw translations of Greek texts into Arabic and worked to make Aristotle compatible with Islamic monotheism. He established the prestige of philosophy in Islamic intellectual culture and wrote on mathematics, optics, and music.

entries.name: Albert the Great
entries.desc: Scholastic; integrated Aristotle, teacher of Aquinas.
entries.tldr: Albertus Magnus was the 13th century's most encyclopedic mind — a Dominican friar who wrote on everything from botany to alchemy while systematically digesting Aristotle for a Christian audience. His main achievement was making Aristotle safe for Christian theology. He was Thomas Aquinas's teacher.

entries.name: Althusser, Louis
entries.tldr: A French Marxist who read Marx as a structuralist. Althusser argued that ideology doesn't distort reality — it constitutes the subjects who inhabit it. His concept of 'ideological state apparatuses' (schools, churches, media) showed how power reproduces itself without violence. His life ended tragically when he murdered his wife during a psychotic episode.

entries.name: Ambedkar, B. R.
entries.desc: Social philosopher of caste and equality; Navayana Buddhism.
entries.tldr: The principal architect of the Indian Constitution, Ambedkar was from the 'untouchable' Mahar caste and made the annihilation of caste the center of his philosophical and political work. He argued caste is graded inequality sanctioned by Hindu religious texts. Late in life he converted to Buddhism, bringing half a million followers, arguing it offered dignity without hierarchy.

entries.name: Amo, Anton Wilhelm
entries.desc: Argued the body, not the mind, bears sensation.
entries.tldr: Born in Ghana, enslaved, brought to Germany as a child gift to a duke, Amo became a philosopher at German universities at a time when this was nearly unimaginable. His philosophical work argued that the human mind has no sensation — sensation belongs only to the body. His life challenges the Eurocentric narrative of philosophy's geography.

entries.name: Anaxagoras
entries.tldr: A pre-Socratic who proposed that everything contains a portion of everything else, and that a cosmic mind (nous) set the universe spinning. He was the first to explain eclipses correctly and was prosecuted for impiety for claiming the sun was a hot rock, not a god. Socrates was a follower.

entries.name: Anaximander
entries.tldr: The first philosopher to write a prose text, Anaximander proposed that the first principle of reality is the 'apeiron' — the boundless or indefinite — rather than any particular substance. He also drew one of the earliest maps of the world and speculated that humans evolved from fish-like creatures.

entries.name: Anaximenes
entries.tldr: The third of the Milesian philosophers, Anaximenes argued that air is the fundamental substance — fire when rarefied, water and earth when condensed. His contribution was linking the mechanism of condensation and rarefaction to observable natural processes.

entries.name: Anscombe, G. E. M.
entries.desc: Intention, action theory; revived virtue ethics.
entries.tldr: One of the most important moral philosophers of the 20th century, Anscombe coined the term 'consequentialism' — as a criticism. Her 1958 paper 'Modern Moral Philosophy' argued that 'moral obligation' divorced from a law-giver makes no sense, and urged a return to Aristotelian virtue ethics. A devout Catholic, she was arrested protesting nuclear weapons.

entries.name: Anselm of Canterbury
entries.tldr: Medieval Archbishop of Canterbury and author of the ontological argument: God is 'that than which nothing greater can be conceived,' and something that exists in reality is greater than something existing only in the mind, therefore God exists. He also formulated the satisfaction theory of atonement that shaped Western Christianity.

entries.name: Appiah, Kwame Anthony
entries.desc: Cosmopolitanism, race, identity, ethics of culture.
entries.tldr: A cosmopolitan philosopher who argues that obligations to strangers are real and that national and ethnic identity shouldn't override them. His work on race argues that racial identities are socially constructed but can still ground legitimate political claims. Knighted in 2023.

entries.name: Aquinas, Thomas
entries.desc: Synthesis of Aristotle and Christianity; natural law.
entries.tldr: The dominant philosopher-theologian of medieval Europe. Aquinas synthesized Aristotle with Christian theology, arguing reason and faith are compatible. His Five Ways attempt to prove God's existence from motion, causation, contingency, degrees of perfection, and design. His Summa Theologica remains a reference point in Catholic thought.

entries.name: Arendt, Hannah
entries.desc: Political theory; totalitarianism, action, banality of evil.
entries.tldr: A political philosopher who thought from the specific horror of 20th-century totalitarianism. Covering the Eichmann trial, she coined 'the banality of evil' — not monsters do evil, but ordinary bureaucrats following orders without thinking. Her The Human Condition distinguished labor, work, and action, arguing political life requires public appearance and speech.

entries.name: Aristotle
entries.desc: Logic, metaphysics, ethics, biology; vast systematic influence.
entries.tldr: The most influential philosopher in Western history. Aristotle founded formal logic, developed systematic ethics and politics, made foundational contributions to biology and physics, and created the first systematic poetics and rhetoric. His 'Golden Mean' — virtue as the midpoint between extremes — shaped moral thinking for millennia.

entries.name: Armstrong, David
entries.desc: Materialist theory of mind; universals, states of affairs.
entries.tldr: Australian materialist who argued that the mind is identical to brain states and that universals are real but immanent — they exist in their instances rather than in a Platonic realm. Clear, rigorous, and influential in metaphysics and philosophy of mind.

entries.name: Aryadeva
entries.tldr: The chief disciple of Nagarjuna and a principal developer of Madhyamaka philosophy. His Catuhsataka argues that all phenomena are empty of intrinsic existence and critiques rival schools. He is particularly sharp on the relationship between philosophical error and suffering.

entries.name: Asanga
entries.tldr: Fourth-century Indian Buddhist philosopher and co-founder of Yogacara ('mind-only') school. He argued that what we take to be an external world is actually a transformation of consciousness — there are no mind-independent objects. Credited with receiving his teachings from Maitreya in a visionary experience.

entries.name: Astell, Mary
entries.tldr: Called the 'first English feminist,' Astell argued in A Serious Proposal to the Ladies (1694) that women's apparent intellectual inferiority was entirely due to lack of education. She proposed a women's college — mocked by men, taken seriously by women. Her feminism came from the theological conviction that women's souls are equal to men's.

entries.name: Atisha
entries.desc: Indian master who revived Buddhism in Tibet.
entries.tldr: Bengali Buddhist master who traveled to Tibet at age 60 and revived Buddhism there. His Lamp for the Path to Enlightenment laid out the graded path (lamrim) that became the template for all subsequent Tibetan Buddhist practice. He emphasized compassion and bodhicitta as the foundation.

entries.name: Augustine of Hippo
entries.desc: Christian Platonism; will, time, original sin.
entries.tldr: The most influential theologian in Western Christianity. His Confessions are the first true autobiography in Western literature. His City of God argues history is the story of two cities: the earthly city of self-love and the heavenly city of love of God. His doctrines of original sin, predestination, and just war shaped Western civilization.

entries.name: Aurelius, Marcus
entries.desc: Stoic emperor; Meditations on virtue and duty.
entries.tldr: Roman Emperor and Stoic philosopher whose Meditations were private notes to himself, never intended for publication. Writing from a position of immense power, he wrote about the discipline of will, acceptance of fate, and the insignificance of fame. One of the most personally honest philosophical texts in any tradition.

entries.name: Aurobindo, Sri
entries.desc: Integral Yoga; spiritual evolution of consciousness.
entries.tldr: An Indian revolutionary who turned philosopher and mystic. He argued evolution does not stop at mind but continues through the supermind — a plane of consciousness beyond ordinary rationality. Spent his last 40 years in Pondicherry developing his Integral Yoga with his French collaborator, the Mother.

entries.name: Averroes (Ibn Rushd)
entries.desc: Great commentator on Aristotle; reason and faith.
entries.tldr: Called 'The Commentator' by medieval Christians because his commentaries on Aristotle were read across Europe for centuries. He defended philosophy against Al-Ghazali and argued reason and revelation, properly understood, cannot conflict. Born in Córdoba, died in Marrakesh.

entries.name: Avicenna (Ibn Sina)
entries.tldr: A Persian polymath who was the dominant philosopher and physician of the medieval Islamic world. His 'Floating Man' thought experiment — imagine a person suspended in space, deprived of all sensation; they would still know they exist — prefigures Descartes's cogito by five centuries. His distinction between essence and existence influenced every subsequent metaphysician.

entries.name: Ayer, A. J.
entries.tldr: The young Oxford philosopher whose Language, Truth and Logic (1936) brought logical positivism to English readers. The verification principle: a statement is meaningful only if it can be empirically verified or is analytically true. Ethics therefore produces meaningless 'pseudo-propositions' — emotional expression, not fact. He later admitted the principle was too strict.

entries.name: Bacon, Francis
entries.tldr: Lord Chancellor of England and the prophet of the scientific method. Bacon argued that knowledge must proceed by systematic observation and induction — not reliance on Aristotle. His 'four idols' named the psychological and social sources of error in human thinking. The founder of empiricism as a methodology.

entries.name: Badiou, Alain
entries.tldr: A French philosopher influenced by set theory, Lacan, and Maoism. For Badiou, being is pure multiplicity — mathematics is ontology. But an 'event' can rupture the normal order, opening new possibility. Truth consists in fidelity to an event. One of the most ambitious systematic philosophers working today.

entries.name: Beauvoir, Simone de
entries.tldr: The founder of modern feminist philosophy. Her The Second Sex (1949) argued that women are made, not born — 'one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.' Social structures shape woman as the Other, defined in relation to man. Her Ethics of Ambiguity is the clearest existentialist ethics written.

entries.name: Benjamin, Walter
entries.tldr: A German-Jewish critic who synthesized Marxism, Jewish messianism, and Frankfurt School critique. His essay on art in the age of mechanical reproduction argued that mass reproduction destroys the 'aura' of artworks. His unfinished Arcades Project was a massive montage of 19th-century Parisian culture. He died by suicide fleeing the Nazis.

entries.name: Bentham, Jeremy
entries.tldr: The founder of utilitarianism. The right action maximizes pleasure and minimizes pain — the 'felicific calculus.' Bentham designed the panopticon, championed decriminalization of homosexuality, and left his body to be preserved as an 'auto-icon' at University College London, where it still sits.

entries.name: Bergson, Henri
entries.tldr: A French philosopher who argued that real time — duration — is not divisible into instants but a continuous, qualitative flow. The intellect spatializes time to control it; intuition grasps the living flow directly. His Creative Evolution argued against mechanistic Darwinism: life is driven by an élan vital. Nobel Prize for Literature in 1927.

entries.name: Berkeley, George
entries.desc: Subjective idealism; “to be is to be perceived.”
entries.tldr: The Irish philosopher who argued, with impeccable logic, that there is no material world. Everything is either a mind or an idea in a mind — 'esse est percipi,' to be is to be perceived. God is the permanent perceiver who keeps things existing when we're not looking. Dr. Johnson's kick of a large stone ('I refute it thus') misses the point.

entries.name: Bhartrhari
entries.tldr: A 5th-century Indian philosopher of language who argued that language is not a human convention but the ultimate nature of reality — Brahman itself is the Word. His concept of sphota holds that a word is a unitary whole grasped all at once, not a sequence of phonemes.

entries.name: Bhaviveka
entries.tldr: A 6th-century Buddhist philosopher who founded the Svatantrika school of Madhyamaka, arguing one must use positive syllogisms to establish emptiness, not just reductio ad absurdum. He wrote critiques of Yogacara and other Buddhist schools and is important for the history of Buddhist logical methodology.

entries.name: Block, Ned
entries.desc: Philosophy of mind; access vs. phenomenal consciousness.
entries.tldr: A philosopher of mind who draws the crucial distinction between 'access consciousness' (information available for reasoning) and 'phenomenal consciousness' (the felt quality of experience). He argues these can come apart, with profound implications for debates about the nature of consciousness.

entries.name: Boethius
entries.desc: Consolation of Philosophy; transmitted logic to medievals.
entries.tldr: A Roman senator who wrote The Consolation of Philosophy while awaiting execution, arguing Fortune's gifts are worthless and true good lies in God. It was the most widely read book in medieval Europe after the Bible. He also translated Aristotle's logic into Latin, making it accessible for centuries.

entries.name: Bonaventure
entries.tldr: Franciscan cardinal and contemporary of Aquinas who chose Augustine over Aristotle. His The Mind's Road to God is a mystical itinerary through creation back to God. He and Aquinas both died in 1274, reputedly within days of each other.

entries.name: Brandom, Robert
entries.tldr: A Pittsburgh pragmatist who argues that the fundamental unit of thought is not the concept but the inference. Meaning is normative — to use a word correctly is to use it in accordance with social norms of inference. His Making It Explicit (1994) is a massive rethinking of semantics and the relationship between mind and world.

entries.name: Brentano, Franz
entries.desc: Intentionality; revived for philosophy of mind.
entries.tldr: An Austrian philosopher whose concept of intentionality — all mental acts are directed at an object — became the founding concept of phenomenology, via his student Husserl. He also pioneered empirical psychology as a discipline. Students include Husserl, Meinong, and (briefly) Freud.

entries.name: Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama)
entries.desc: Founder of Buddhism; Four Noble Truths, anatta.
entries.tldr: A 5th-century BCE Indian prince who renounced comfort to seek liberation from suffering. The Four Noble Truths: suffering exists; it arises from craving; it can cease; there is a path. The doctrine of anatta (no-self) argues there is no permanent soul — only a stream of interdependent processes. The most influential teacher in Asian history.

entries.name: Buddhaghosa
entries.tldr: A 5th-century Theravada monk who wrote the Visuddhimagga ('Path of Purification'), the most comprehensive account of Buddhist meditation and psychology in the Theravada tradition. A detailed map of the mental factors, stages of concentration, and insight practices that lead to nirvana.

entries.name: Burge, Tyler
entries.tldr: A UCLA philosopher whose 1979 thought experiment 'Arthritis' established content externalism for mental states. If you use the word 'arthritis' differently from the experts, your thought content includes what the experts mean, not just what's in your head. Mental content is partly constituted by one's linguistic community.

entries.name: Butler, Joseph
entries.desc: Conscience, moral psychology; critique of egoism.
entries.tldr: An 18th-century bishop who was one of the most careful moral psychologists before Kant. Against Hobbes, he argued human beings have genuine benevolent impulses — we are not just disguised egoists. His conscience carries intrinsic authority to approve or disapprove of actions.

entries.name: Butler, Judith
entries.tldr: The most cited living gender theorist. Her Gender Trouble (1990) argued that gender is not an expression of a prior natural identity but is performatively constituted — we enact gender through repeated acts that produce the illusion of a stable inner essence. There is no 'real woman' behind the performance.

entries.name: Camus, Albert
entries.desc: The absurd; revolt, ethics of life.
entries.tldr: A French-Algerian writer and philosopher who made the absurd — the confrontation between human need for meaning and the world's silence — the center of his work. The Myth of Sisyphus argues we must imagine Sisyphus happy. The Rebel argues revolutionary violence betrays the rebel's own humanism. He died in a car accident in 1960.

entries.name: Carnap, Rudolf
entries.tldr: The most technically rigorous of the Vienna Circle logical positivists. Carnap argued that many traditional philosophical questions are meaningless pseudoproblems arising from confusion about language. His sharp separation of analytic from synthetic was later challenged by Quine.

entries.name: Carneades
entries.desc: Academic Skeptic; probabilism, critique of dogmatism.
entries.tldr: The most formidable Academic Skeptic, Carneades scandalized Rome by arguing on successive days for and against justice — demonstrating that philosophical argument can support any conclusion. He developed 'probabilism': we cannot know anything certain, but some impressions are more convincing and can guide action. He wrote nothing.

entries.name: Cavell, Stanley
entries.tldr: A Harvard philosopher working at the boundary of philosophy, literature, and film. Influenced by Wittgenstein and Austin, he argued that ordinary language philosophy reveals the human form of life. His readings of Hollywood comedies as philosophical texts were groundbreaking.

entries.name: Cavendish, Margaret
entries.tldr: The Duchess of Newcastle developed a vitalist materialism: nature is constituted by a self-moving matter that is itself alive and self-knowing. Against mechanical philosophy, she argued mechanism couldn't explain life or perception. She also wrote the first science fiction novel, The Blazing World (1666).

entries.name: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu
entries.tldr: A 15th-century Bengali saint-philosopher who founded Gaudiya Vaishnavism. His doctrine of achintya-bhedabheda ('inconceivable difference-and-non-difference') holds that the relationship between soul and Brahman is simultaneously one and different. His ecstatic devotion to Krishna became the foundation of ISKCON.

entries.name: Chalmers, David
entries.desc: Framed the “hard problem” of consciousness; property dualism.
entries.tldr: An Australian philosopher who in 1995 formulated 'the hard problem of consciousness' — why does any physical process give rise to subjective experience at all? We can explain the functional correlates of consciousness, but explaining why there is something it is like to see red remains deeply mysterious.

entries.name: Chandrakirti
entries.tldr: A 7th-century Madhyamaka philosopher who became the definitive interpreter of Nagarjuna for the Prasangika school. He argued the correct method for establishing emptiness is purely negative: expose the absurdities in the opponent's position. His Madhyamakavatara is the most studied Madhyamaka text in Tibetan Buddhism.

entries.name: Cheng Hao
entries.tldr: One of two Cheng brothers who laid the foundations of Neo-Confucian philosophy in the Song dynasty. Cheng Hao emphasized the unity of self and world: the person of ren (humaneness) experiences no boundary between self and things. He is a precursor of the School of Mind.

entries.name: Cheng Yi
entries.tldr: Cheng Hao's younger brother and the more systematic philosopher of the pair. He emphasized the investigation of li (principle) in things as the path to moral knowledge, laying the groundwork for Zhu Xi's School of Principle. His austere, precise style contrasts with his brother's more mystical approach.

entries.name: Chomsky, Noam
entries.tldr: The most influential linguist of the 20th century. Chomsky argued that children's language acquisition requires an innate 'universal grammar' hardwired into human cognition — the 'poverty of the stimulus' argument. This helped launch the cognitive revolution. In politics, he is a tireless anarchist critic of American foreign policy.

entries.name: Chrysippus
entries.desc: Second founder of Stoicism; propositional logic.
entries.tldr: The third head of the Stoic school and effectively its systematizer — 'if there were no Chrysippus, there would be no Stoa.' He developed Stoic propositional logic (effectively rediscovered in the 19th century) and articulated the theory of fate and free will. His over 700 books survive only in fragments.

entries.name: Churchland, Patricia
entries.tldr: A Canadian neurophilosopher who has spent her career arguing that philosophy of mind must be done in close partnership with neuroscience. Her Neurophilosophy (1986) argued that folk psychological categories like belief and desire will eventually be replaced by neuroscientific ones.

entries.name: Churchland, Paul
entries.tldr: The boldest proponent of eliminative materialism — the view that folk psychology (beliefs, desires, intentions) is a radically false theory that will be replaced by a completed neuroscience. He argues that connectionist neural networks, not propositional attitudes, are the correct model of cognition.

entries.name: Cicero
entries.desc: Roman eclectic; transmitted Greek philosophy, natural law.
entries.tldr: The great Roman orator who transmitted Greek philosophy to Rome through elegant Latin prose. An eclectic Academic Skeptic, he wrote on ethics, political philosophy, and the nature of the gods while insisting probable reasoning guides action even where certainty is impossible. His works on natural law influenced medieval political thought.

entries.name: Cixous, Hélène
entries.tldr: An Algerian-French theorist who coined 'écriture féminine' — writing from the female body and libido — as a challenge to phallocentric language. She draws on psychoanalysis and Derrida to argue for a writing practice that embraces multiplicity. Her essay 'The Laugh of the Medusa' is a landmark feminist manifesto.

entries.name: Comte, Auguste
entries.tldr: The founder of positivism and sociology. Comte's 'law of three stages' held that every science passes through theological, metaphysical, and positive stages. In his later life he developed a 'Religion of Humanity' worshipping great thinkers — a secular church that convinced almost nobody.

entries.name: Confucius
entries.tldr: The founder of China's dominant ethical tradition. Confucius argued society is harmonized through virtue (de), ritual propriety (li), and humaneness (ren). He believed ancient Zhou ritual practices embodied these values and that recovering them would restore social order. He trained students in the art of government and ethical living.

entries.name: Conway, Anne
entries.tldr: A 17th-century English philosopher who corresponded with Leibniz (who read and was influenced by her work). Her Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy argues for a vitalist monism: all reality is a single substance, spirit-matter, capable of transformation. She was a Quaker and suffered severe headaches her entire adult life.

entries.name: Crescas, Hasdai
entries.desc: Critique of Aristotelian physics; divine infinity.
entries.tldr: A Catalan Jewish philosopher who attacked Aristotle's physics. He argued infinite space and infinite time are coherent, and his critique of Aristotle's proofs for God's existence influenced Spinoza. He argued for a determinism compatible with divine command — humans act from love of God, not libertarian free will.

entries.name: Dai Zhen
entries.desc: Qing evidential scholar; critiqued Neo-Confucian metaphysics.
entries.tldr: A Qing dynasty evidential scholar who criticized Neo-Confucian metaphysics as Buddhist influence in disguise. He argued that li (principle) is simply the pattern within qi (material force), not a transcendent reality. He argued Neo-Confucian moral rigorism suppressed natural human desires and became a tool of oppression.

entries.name: Davidson, Donald
entries.tldr: An American philosopher famous for 'anomalous monism' — mental events are identical to physical events but mental kinds don't reduce to physical kinds — and truth-conditional semantics. His thought experiment about 'radical interpretation' — working out what a community's words mean from scratch — influenced epistemology and philosophy of language.

entries.name: Daya Krishna
entries.tldr: An Indian philosopher who argued for 'comparative philosophy' not as synthesis but as genuine dialogue between traditions. He challenged both the Western assumption that it alone has 'philosophy' and the Indian assumption that Western methods are irrelevant. His approach respects differences without ranking them.

entries.name: Deleuze, Gilles
entries.tldr: A French philosopher of difference and multiplicity who resisted the Western tradition's tendency to privilege identity and sameness. With Félix Guattari he introduced concepts like the rhizome (a non-hierarchical network model for thought and culture) and wrote major studies of Hume, Spinoza, Nietzsche, and Bergson.

entries.name: Democritus
entries.desc: Atomism; reality is atoms and void.
entries.tldr: The ancient atomist who proposed that everything consists of tiny, indivisible particles (atoms) moving through void — a bold materialist conjecture without experimental support that anticipates modern physics. He also had an elaborate ethics recommending cheerfulness as the highest good, to be achieved through moderation.

entries.name: Dennett, Daniel
entries.tldr: The most publicly influential American philosopher of mind of his generation. Dennett argued that consciousness is not a mysterious inner theater but a process that wins out in neural competition. He denied qualia in the strong sense and explained apparent intentionality through his 'intentional stance.' Co-founder of the modern naturalistic tradition in philosophy of mind.

entries.name: Derrida, Jacques
entries.tldr: The founder of deconstruction, Derrida argued that Western philosophy has always privileged presence and identity while suppressing absence and difference. His close readings show canonical texts undermine their own central claims. Différance — a pun on deferring and differing — names the endless play of signification that makes fixed meaning impossible.

entries.name: Descartes, René
entries.tldr: The father of modern philosophy. His method of radical doubt led to the cogito ('I think, therefore I am') as the one indubitable truth. He argued mind and body are distinct substances (Cartesian dualism), creating the 'mind-body problem' that has driven philosophy of mind ever since. He died in Stockholm from pneumonia contracted while teaching Christina of Sweden at 5am.

entries.name: Dewey, John
entries.tldr: The great American pragmatist philosopher of democracy and education. Dewey argued that thinking is a form of problem-solving, not the contemplation of abstract truths. His laboratory school demonstrated that education works best as structured experience rather than passive reception. Democracy is not just a political system but a mode of associated living.

entries.name: Dharmakirti
entries.tldr: The greatest Buddhist logician. He argued that valid cognition comes in only two types: direct perception and inference. His apoha theory explains concept formation without positing universals. His writings remain central to Tibetan Buddhist philosophical education.

entries.name: Diderot, Denis
entries.tldr: Chief editor of the Encyclopédie — the great Enlightenment project compiling all human knowledge. A materialist who argued that matter can become sentient given sufficient organization, foreshadowing modern biology. Friend of Voltaire, Rousseau, and Hume.

entries.name: Dignaga
entries.desc: Founder of Buddhist logic; apoha theory of meaning.
entries.tldr: The founder of Buddhist logic in the 5th century. He created a systematic theory of valid knowledge with perception and inference as its two sources. His apoha theory — concepts function by excluding their opposite — solved the problem of universals without reifying them.

entries.name: Diogenes of Sinope
entries.desc: Founder figure of Cynicism; ascetic provocation.
entries.tldr: The original cynic philosopher who lived in a barrel, urinated on people who insulted him, and told Alexander the Great to get out of his sunlight. Behind the provocations was a serious philosophy: virtue consists in living according to nature, not social convention. All social distinctions are 'false coinage.' Called 'Socrates gone mad.'

entries.name: Dogen
entries.tldr: The 13th-century Japanese Zen master whose Shobogenzo is the most philosophically sophisticated text in Zen literature. Dogen argued that practice and enlightenment are not means and end but are identical — sitting zazen just-sitting is already enlightenment. His treatment of time ('Being-Time') argues each moment is the whole of time.

entries.name: Dong Zhongshu
entries.tldr: The 2nd-century BCE Han Confucian who institutionalized Confucianism as state ideology. His cosmology held that human society, nature, and heaven are correlated systems — disasters signal Heaven's displeasure with the ruler. He gave Confucianism its political form for two millennia.

entries.name: Du Bois, W. E. B.
entries.tldr: The greatest African American intellectual of the 20th century. His concept of 'double consciousness' — being both American and Black, seeing yourself through a hostile white gaze — is one of the most important formulations in American philosophy. He helped found the NAACP and died in Ghana the day before the March on Washington.

entries.name: Du Châtelet, Émilie
entries.desc: Translated Newton's Principia; metaphysics of force.
entries.tldr: A French mathematician and physicist who produced the definitive French translation of Newton's Principia and introduced Leibniz's concept of vis viva to correct Newton's physics. One of the most important natural philosophers of the 18th century, long overshadowed by her relationship with Voltaire.

entries.name: Dummett, Michael
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher who argued the fundamental question of metaphysics — realism vs. anti-realism — should be framed in terms of meaning and logic. Anti-realists should use intuitionistic logic, rejecting the law of excluded middle where verification is unavailable. He was also a historian of analytic philosophy and a passionate anti-racist activist.

entries.name: Duns Scotus, John
entries.tldr: The 'Subtle Doctor' — a Scottish Franciscan whose fine distinctions and complex arguments rival Aquinas. His concept of 'haecceity' (thisness) explains individual identity. His doctrine of the univocity of being — being applies to God and creatures in the same sense — broke with Aquinas and influenced Hegel.

entries.name: Dussel, Enrique
entries.tldr: An Argentine-Mexican philosopher who developed the 'philosophy of liberation' from the standpoint of Latin America as 'the underside of modernity.' Against Eurocentrism, he argues modernity was constituted through the conquest and exclusion of the Other — indigenous peoples, enslaved Africans.

entries.name: Dworkin, Ronald
entries.desc: Law as integrity; rights, political morality.
entries.tldr: A legal and moral philosopher who argued against legal positivism: law is not merely what officials say but includes the principles needed to make best sense of legal practice. Rights 'trump' consequentialist calculations. His Taking Rights Seriously and Law's Empire are the most influential works in Anglo-American philosophy of law.

entries.name: Eckhart, Meister
entries.desc: Christian mysticism; ground of the soul.
entries.tldr: A German Dominican mystic who preached that the soul's 'ground' is identical to God's ground — at the deepest level, there is no distinction between soul and divine. He preached in German, making mystical theology accessible. Many positions were condemned by the Pope after his death.

entries.name: Elisabeth of Bohemia
entries.tldr: A 17th-century German princess who carried on a famous correspondence with Descartes that revealed a decisive problem: how can an immaterial mind cause changes in an extended body? Descartes's answer was unsatisfying, and the problem she identified remains one of philosophy's hardest. Leibniz rated her more highly than Descartes.

entries.name: Empedocles
entries.tldr: A 5th-century BCE Sicilian philosopher-shaman who proposed that all matter is composed of four 'roots' — fire, water, earth, air — combined and separated by cosmic forces of Love and Strife. He reportedly jumped into Mount Etna to prove his divinity. He introduced an early evolutionary account of living things as combinations that either succeed or fail.

entries.name: Epictetus
entries.desc: Stoic; dichotomy of control, ethics of self-mastery.
entries.tldr: A Stoic philosopher who was born a slave in Rome. He taught that freedom is entirely internal: the only thing truly ours is our response to events. His Discourses and Enchiridion present Stoic practice as a daily discipline of distinguishing what is 'up to us' from what is not. Marcus Aurelius read him constantly.

entries.name: Epicurus
entries.tldr: The founder of the Garden school, Epicurus taught that the highest good is ataraxia — tranquility free from fear and pain. The key fears to overcome are fear of the gods and fear of death ('when death is, I am not; when I am, death is not'). His atomic materialism was entirely in service of this therapeutic goal. Often mischaracterized as a pleasure-seeker.

entries.name: Eriugena, John Scotus
entries.tldr: The most original philosopher of the Carolingian era, a Neoplatonist who translated Greek theological texts for Charlemagne's successors. His Periphyseon divides all reality into four categories that trace the cosmic circuit from God through creation and back to God.

entries.name: Fanon, Frantz
entries.tldr: A Martinican psychiatrist and revolutionary philosopher whose The Wretched of the Earth (1961) was the bible of decolonization. He argued colonial violence damages not only the colonized's body but their psyche. His Black Skin, White Masks is a phenomenological analysis of the experience of blackness.

entries.name: Feyerabend, Paul
entries.tldr: The 'worst enemy of science' (his own description). Against Popper and Lakatos, Feyerabend argued in Against Method that there is no single method that guarantees scientific progress — 'anything goes.' Science is one tradition among many, not necessarily superior to others.

entries.name: Fichte, Johann Gottlieb
entries.tldr: The first post-Kantian idealist who radicalized Kant by making the self the absolute first principle — the 'I posits itself.' His Addresses to the German Nation (1807-8) articulated a nationalism in response to Napoleon's conquest. He died at 51 from typhus contracted during the wars.

entries.name: Fine, Kit
entries.tldr: A British philosopher who has revitalized metaphysics through work on essence, grounding, and ontology. His 'new theory of essence' argues that a thing's essential properties explain why it has the modal properties it does. His work on grounding has been central to contemporary metaphysics.

entries.name: Fodor, Jerry
entries.desc: Language of thought; modularity of mind.
entries.tldr: An American philosopher who argued for the 'language of thought' hypothesis — thinking is computing over mental representations analogous to a language. The mind has a modular architecture: domain-specific input systems are informationally encapsulated and fast; central cognition is slow and global.

entries.name: Foot, Philippa
entries.desc: Virtue ethics; trolley problem, natural goodness.
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher who co-invented the trolley problem and was one of the founders of modern virtue ethics. Her Natural Goodness argues that ethical evaluations of humans are the same kind as evaluations of other living things — not performing their natural function is bad in both cases.

entries.name: Foucault, Michel
entries.tldr: One of the most influential thinkers of the 20th century. Foucault's 'genealogies' traced how modern institutions — the clinic, the prison, the asylum — don't just repress but produce their subjects. Power is not just repressive but productive; it generates knowledge, norms, and subjectivities.

entries.name: Frege, Gottlob
entries.desc: Founder of modern logic; sense and reference.
entries.tldr: The founder of modern logic and one of the founders of analytic philosophy. His Begriffsschrift (1879) created the first complete logical system — predicate calculus — surpassing Aristotle's logic in one step. His distinction between sense and reference is a cornerstone of philosophy of language.

entries.name: Fricker, Miranda
entries.desc: Epistemic injustice; testimonial and hermeneutical injustice.
entries.tldr: A British philosopher who coined 'epistemic injustice': testimonial injustice (believed less because you're Black or a woman) and hermeneutical injustice (lacking the concepts to understand your own experience — what happened to victims of workplace harassment before it had a name). Combines analytic epistemology with feminist political philosophy.

entries.name: Gadamer, Hans-Georg
entries.tldr: The founder of philosophical hermeneutics. His Truth and Method argued that understanding is not a method we apply to texts but a mode of being. Understanding happens when our 'fore-structures' fuse with the text's horizon in a 'fusion of horizons.' He quarreled famously with Derrida about whether understanding is always possible.

entries.name: Gandhi, Mohandas
entries.tldr: India's most important political philosopher. Gandhi developed satyagraha ('truth-force') as a method of nonviolent resistance, arguing means and ends are inseparable — violent means corrupt any just end. His critique of modern civilization rejected Western industrialism in favor of village self-sufficiency. He was shot dead in 1948 by a Hindu nationalist.

entries.name: Gangesa Upadhyaya
entries.tldr: The founder of Navya-Nyaya ('new logic'), a 14th-century school that revolutionized Indian logic by developing a precise technical vocabulary for expressing logical relations. His Tattvacintamani is among the most technically demanding texts in Indian philosophy.

entries.name: Gaudapada
entries.tldr: The teacher of Shankara's teacher and a pivotal figure in early Advaita Vedanta. He used the states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep to argue that ultimate reality is pure consciousness — undivided, unchanging. His controversial use of Buddhist arguments to establish Advaita positions earned accusations of crypto-Buddhism.

entries.name: Gautama, Aksapada
entries.desc: Reputed founder of Nyaya logic; Nyaya Sutras.
entries.tldr: The reputed founder of the Nyaya school, author of the Nyaya Sutras — a systematic treatise on sources of knowledge, inference, and the things to be known. Nyaya is unusual in Indian philosophy for its emphasis on debate, refutation, and the norms of correct reasoning.

entries.name: Gersonides
entries.desc: Jewish Aristotelian; divine foreknowledge and free will.
entries.tldr: A 14th-century Provençal Jewish philosopher who argued that God does not know future contingent facts (which would preclude free will). His Milhamot Ha-Shem takes on Maimonides and Averroes with Aristotelian logic applied to Jewish theology. One of the most rigorous medieval Jewish thinkers.

entries.name: Gettier, Edmund
entries.tldr: Famous for writing one of the shortest and most consequential papers in philosophy — three pages in 1963 demolishing the traditional definition of knowledge as 'justified true belief.' His counterexamples launched a half-century of epistemological debate still ongoing. He published almost nothing else.

entries.name: Gorgias
entries.tldr: The greatest Sophist, Gorgias argued (perhaps ironically) that nothing exists; if anything exists, it cannot be known; if it can be known, it cannot be communicated. He was interested in the power of rhetoric over belief. Plato wrote a dialogue against him.

entries.name: Gramsci, Antonio
entries.tldr: An Italian Marxist who wrote his most important work in prison under Mussolini. His concept of 'hegemony' — dominant classes maintain power through cultural consent rather than just force — transformed Marxist political theory. He argued revolution in the West required cultural struggle before direct political action.

entries.name: Grosseteste, Robert
entries.tldr: An English bishop who proposed an early account of the scientific method including controlled experimentation. More importantly, he was one of the key transmitters of ancient learning to medieval Europe through translations of Aristotle and Greek science.

entries.name: Guo Xiang
entries.tldr: A 3rd-century commentator on the Zhuangzi whose commentary became so authoritative it was often read as the text itself. His interpretation 'naturalizes' Daoism — things transform spontaneously without any external Dao causing them. Each thing self-produces its own nature.

entries.name: Gyekye, Kwame
entries.tldr: A Ghanaian philosopher who developed African communitarian ethics from Akan concepts. His 'moderate communitarianism' preserves individual rights and critical agency while taking community seriously. His concept of personhood in African thought influenced debates in African philosophy.

entries.name: Gödel, Kurt
entries.tldr: The 20th century's greatest logician. His incompleteness theorems (1931) proved that any consistent formal system capable of expressing basic arithmetic contains true statements that cannot be proved within the system. This shattered Hilbert's program to formalize all mathematics. He was Einstein's closest friend at Princeton.

entries.name: Haack, Susan
entries.tldr: An American philosopher known for 'foundherentism' in epistemology — a hybrid of foundationalism and coherentism, like a crossword puzzle where entries support each other and fit the grid. She has also been a persistent defender of scientific integrity against postmodern challenges to truth and objectivity.

entries.name: Habermas, Jürgen
entries.desc: Communicative action; discourse ethics, public sphere.
entries.tldr: The leading figure of the second generation of the Frankfurt School, Habermas departed from Adorno's pessimism to develop communicative rationality. Reason is embedded in the norms presupposed by sincere discourse. His discourse ethics grounds moral norms in the conditions of ideal communication.

entries.name: Han Fei
entries.desc: Leading Legalist theorist of statecraft and law.
entries.tldr: The leading theorist of Chinese Legalism, opposed to Confucian moralism. Han Fei argued that rulers should govern through clear laws, strict punishments, and administrative technique — not virtue or moral exhortation. His writings became the handbook for the Qin state's unification of China.

entries.name: Hare, R. M.
entries.tldr: An Oxford moral philosopher who developed 'universal prescriptivism' — moral judgments are prescriptions that must be universalizable. This combines the Kantian universalizability criterion with a non-cognitivist analysis of moral language.

entries.name: Haribhadra
entries.tldr: An 8th-century Jain philosopher who surveyed all major Indian philosophical schools with remarkable fairness. His anekantavada approach holds that reality can be described from many perspectives, each capturing something true but none capturing everything.

entries.name: Harman, Gilbert
entries.desc: Inference to best explanation; moral relativism.
entries.tldr: A Princeton philosopher who contributed to epistemology (formalizing inference to the best explanation) and moral philosophy (arguing for moral relativism). He drew a sharp distinction between inner judgments requiring shared norms and outer judgments from outside the agent's community.

entries.name: Haslanger, Sally
entries.desc: Social construction of gender and race; ameliorative analysis.
entries.tldr: An MIT philosopher known for 'ameliorative analysis' — rather than analysing concepts as they are, ask what concepts we need for our purposes. Applied to gender and race, she argues these are real social positions defined by structural relations of power and hierarchy.

entries.name: Hayashi Razan
entries.desc: Tokugawa Zhu Xi Neo-Confucian; adviser to the shogunate.
entries.tldr: The Confucian adviser to the first three Tokugawa shoguns who established the Zhu Xi tradition as the official state orthodoxy in Japan. His institutional legacy — the Shoheiko academy — trained samurai bureaucrats for generations.

entries.name: Hegel, G. W. F.
entries.desc: Absolute idealism; dialectic, phenomenology of spirit.
entries.tldr: One of the most ambitious and difficult philosophers who ever lived. Hegel's Phenomenology of Spirit traces consciousness's journey to absolute knowing. His dialectic holds that history is the self-development of Spirit through contradiction and reconciliation. Marx took the dialectic and turned it materialist. Almost every major 19th and 20th-century philosopher was either developing or reacting against Hegel.

entries.name: Heidegger, Martin
entries.desc: Being and Time; fundamental ontology, Dasein.
entries.tldr: The most influential European philosopher of the 20th century and one of its most controversial. Being and Time (1927) argued that Western philosophy had forgotten 'the question of Being.' Dasein ('being-there') is always already in a world, with others, toward death. His later work argued technology reveals the world as mere 'standing reserve.' He was a Nazi and never fully recanted.

entries.name: Hemachandra
entries.tldr: A 12th-century Jain monk who was the court philosopher of the Chaulukya kings of Gujarat. His Yogashastra is a comprehensive manual for Jain practice. An encyclopedic grammarian, logician, and historian, he helped make Gujarat briefly united under the Jain ideal of ahimsa.

entries.name: Heraclitus
entries.tldr: A 6th-century BCE Greek philosopher known as 'the Obscure.' 'You cannot step into the same river twice.' Everything flows. The logos — the rational principle governing all change — unifies opposites. He believed fire was the basic element. He despised most people for their lack of philosophical sense.

entries.name: Herder, Johann Gottfried
entries.tldr: An 18th-century German philosopher who challenged Enlightenment universalism. Different nations have different but equally valuable cultures and spirits (Volkgeist). Language is not just a tool for thought but constitutes it. His influence on Romanticism, nationalism, and the philosophy of culture was profound.

entries.name: Hildegard of Bingen
entries.tldr: A 12th-century German abbess who received visions she described in theological and natural philosophical writings. She also composed extraordinary music and invented a secret language. A charismatic preacher and political correspondent to popes and emperors.

entries.name: Hobbes, Thomas
entries.tldr: The founder of modern political philosophy. Hobbes argued that in the state of nature, life is 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short' — so people rationally agree to surrender their liberty to a sovereign in exchange for protection. The sovereign's authority is absolute. Leviathan (1651) was the most rigorous argument for absolutism ever written.

entries.name: Horkheimer, Max
entries.desc: Frankfurt School; critical theory, instrumental reason.
entries.tldr: Director of the Frankfurt Institute who, with Adorno, wrote Dialectic of Enlightenment (1947). They argued that instrumental reason — reason as a tool for domination — had consumed Enlightenment emancipatory reason, leading to fascism and the culture industry.

entries.name: Hountondji, Paulin
entries.desc: Critique of ethnophilosophy; African scientific thought.
entries.tldr: A Beninese philosopher who argued that what is called 'African philosophy' by ethnologists — the folk wisdom and myths of various peoples — is not philosophy in any serious sense. This 'ethnophilosophy' patronizes Africans by denying them the capacity for critical thought. The debate this provoked shaped the field for decades.

entries.name: Hume, David
entries.tldr: The Scottish Enlightenment's greatest philosopher and arguably the most important philosopher to write in English. Hume argued all ideas derive from impressions; causation is a habit of mind; the argument from design doesn't work; miracles are always less credible than testimony for them. The is-ought problem: you can't derive moral claims from factual ones.

entries.name: Husserl, Edmund
entries.tldr: The founder of phenomenology. Husserl's method involves the epoché — bracketing assumptions about the external world to study pure experience. Intentionality — consciousness is always consciousness of something — is the fundamental structure. His students (Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, Sartre) transformed phenomenology in directions he didn't always recognize.

entries.name: Hypatia
entries.desc: Neoplatonist; mathematician and philosopher of Alexandria.
entries.tldr: The first female philosopher whose life and work are well documented. Head of the Neoplatonist school in Alexandria, she was a mathematician, astronomer, and philosopher. She was murdered by a Christian mob in 415 — torn apart in a political conflict. Her own philosophical writings are lost.

entries.name: Iamblichus
entries.tldr: A Syrian Neoplatonist who disagreed with Plotinus's intellectualism, arguing that ritual and 'theurgy' — divine action through material forms — is necessary for the soul's ascent. He influenced the later Neoplatonists who shaped pagan resistance to Christianity.

entries.name: Ibn Arabi
entries.desc: Sufi metaphysics; unity of being (wahdat al-wujud).
entries.tldr: The greatest mystic philosopher in the Islamic tradition. His doctrine of wahdat al-wujud ('unity of being') argues that all of reality is a self-disclosure of the one God. His Fusus al-Hikam gives a mystical interpretation of the prophets. Enormously influential on later Sufism and Islamic philosophy.

entries.name: Ibn Khaldun
entries.desc: Founder of historiography and sociology; asabiyya.
entries.tldr: A 14th-century North African philosopher often called the founder of sociology and economics. His Muqaddimah develops a cyclical theory of history based on asabiyya — group solidarity. Desert peoples with strong asabiyya conquer settled empires, then become soft and are conquered in turn. A rigorous empirical social science 400 years before its time.

entries.name: Ibn Tufayl
entries.desc: Philosophical novel Hayy ibn Yaqzan; autodidactic reason.
entries.tldr: A 12th-century Andalusian philosopher whose Hayy ibn Yaqzan tells of a child raised alone on a desert island who discovers the full truth of philosophy and mysticism through pure reason, without teachers or revelation. This thought experiment argues for the compatibility of reason and religion. Influenced Robinson Crusoe.

entries.name: Iqbal, Muhammad
entries.tldr: The poet-philosopher of Pakistan who developed a modern Islamic philosophy. Influenced by Bergson and Nietzsche, he argued for a dynamic self (khudi) that realizes itself through action, against fatalism and passivity. He believed Islamic thought needed to renew itself from within.

entries.name: Irigaray, Luce
entries.tldr: A Belgian-French feminist philosopher who argued Western philosophy has been structured by a 'phallogocentric' logic that reduces woman to the negative of man. She is interested in developing a philosophical account of sexual difference that affirms women's specificity rather than seeking androgynous equality.

entries.name: Ishvarakrishna
entries.desc: Author of Samkhyakarika, earliest systematic Samkhya.
entries.tldr: The author of the Samkhyakarika, the earliest surviving systematic presentation of Samkhya philosophy. Samkhya posits two fundamental realities: Purusha (pure consciousness) and Prakriti (primordial matter). Liberation consists in Purusha realizing its complete distinctness from Prakriti.

entries.name: Jackson, Frank
entries.tldr: An Australian philosopher famous for the 'Mary's room' thought experiment. Mary is a neuroscientist who knows all physical facts about color vision but has lived in a black-and-white room. When she first sees red, does she learn something new? Jackson argued yes — establishing that physicalism cannot account for qualia. He later changed his mind.

entries.name: James, William
entries.desc: Pragmatism; truth, will to believe, psychology.
entries.tldr: The great American pragmatist psychologist-philosopher. Truth, for James, is what works — ideas become true insofar as they help us get into satisfactory relations with experience. His Will to Believe argued we are entitled to believe certain propositions on non-evidential grounds. His Varieties of Religious Experience remains the best empirical study of mystical experience.

entries.name: Jaspers, Karl
entries.tldr: A German philosopher who developed an 'existenz philosophy' centered on 'limit situations' — death, suffering, struggle, guilt — which reveal our freedom when we cannot escape them. He wrote On the Question of German Guilt (1946) analyzing Germany's responsibility for the Holocaust.

entries.name: Jayanta Bhatta
entries.tldr: A 9th-century Kashmiri Naiyayika who defended Nyaya realism against both Buddhist logic and Mimamsa alternatives. His Nyayamanjari surveys all major schools of Indian philosophy and is an invaluable source for the history of ideas.

entries.name: Jinul
entries.desc: Founder of Korean Jogye Seon; sudden enlightenment.
entries.tldr: The founder of the Jogye order and the father of Korean Buddhism, Jinul advocated 'sudden enlightenment, gradual cultivation.' He synthesized the meditative Seon (Chan) tradition with the doctrinal Hwaeom (Huayan) tradition, creating a distinctively Korean Buddhist philosophy.

entries.name: Judah Halevi
entries.desc: Kuzari; critique of philosophy, defense of revelation.
entries.tldr: A 12th-century Spanish Jewish poet and philosopher whose Kuzari is a defense of Judaism through a fictional dialogue. The king of the Khazars tries philosophers, Christians, and Muslims before a rabbi — and converts to Judaism. Halevi argues genuine religious truth is transmitted through historical experience, not reason alone.

entries.name: Kamalashila
entries.tldr: An 8th-century Indian Buddhist philosopher who defended the graduated-path tradition against 'sudden enlightenment' in the Samye Debate in Tibet. His Bhavanakrama ('Stages of Meditation') established the Indian model as Tibet's official path and shaped Tibetan Buddhism's methodical approach.

entries.name: Kanada
entries.tldr: The reputed founder of the Vaisheshika school, whose Vaisheshika Sutras propose a realist, atomistic ontology: the world consists of eternal, indivisible atoms that combine to form all things. His categories — substance, quality, action, universal, particular, inherence — are an early attempt at a complete ontological scheme.

entries.name: Kant, Immanuel
entries.tldr: The central figure in Western philosophy since his own time. Kant's 'Copernican revolution' argued that objects conform to our modes of cognition — space, time, and the categories are structures of mind that make experience possible, not features of things-in-themselves. The categorical imperative grounded ethics in pure reason. His third Critique connected aesthetics and teleology.

entries.name: Kierkegaard, Søren
entries.desc: Father of existentialism; faith, subjectivity, anxiety.
entries.tldr: The first existentialist and one of the most original philosophers in any tradition. Writing under pseudonyms, Kierkegaard described three 'stages' of existence: aesthetic (pleasure), ethical (duty), and religious (the 'leap of faith' beyond reason). His Fear and Trembling analyzes Abraham's command to sacrifice Isaac as the model of genuine faith.

entries.name: Korsgaard, Christine
entries.tldr: A Harvard philosopher who develops a Kantian constructivism — moral norms are binding because we construct them through practical reason as conditions of our agency. Her Sources of Normativity traces why moral claims make a claim on us. Her Self-Constitution argues ethical principles are the principles of constituting oneself as a unified agent.

entries.name: Kripke, Saul
entries.desc: Naming and Necessity; rigid designation, modal logic.
entries.tldr: A logician and philosopher who revolutionized philosophy of language with Naming and Necessity (1970). Names are 'rigid designators' — they refer to the same object in every possible world. He showed that 'Water is H₂O' is a necessary truth discovered empirically. He also developed possible worlds semantics for modal logic.

entries.name: Krishnamurti, Jiddu
entries.desc: Freedom, thought, the nature of mind.
entries.tldr: Raised to be the World Teacher of a theosophical movement, Krishnamurti dissolved the organization and declared 'truth is a pathless land' — no teacher, tradition, or method can deliver it. His subsequent teachings emphasize direct observation of consciousness without the interference of thought or tradition.

entries.name: Kristeva, Julia
entries.tldr: A Bulgarian-French theorist who combined semiotics, psychoanalysis, and feminism. Her concept of the 'semiotic' — a pre-linguistic layer of rhythm, tone, and drive — disrupts the stable 'symbolic' order of the subject. Her Powers of Horror analyzes 'abjection' — the exclusion of bodily fluids and the maternal body that constitutes the subject.

entries.name: Kuhn, Thomas
entries.desc: Paradigm shifts; structure of scientific revolutions.
entries.tldr: A historian of science whose Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962) transformed how everyone thinks about scientific change. Normal science works within a 'paradigm.' Anomalies accumulate; a 'scientific revolution' replaces it with a new incommensurable paradigm. His argument that paradigms are replaced in sociological events rather than simply falsified was hugely controversial.

entries.name: Kukai
entries.desc: Founder of Japanese Shingon esoteric Buddhism.
entries.tldr: The founder of Shingon Buddhism in Japan and one of Japan's most celebrated cultural figures. His philosophy of 'Immediate realization in this very body' argued enlightenment can be attained in a single lifetime through esoteric practices. Also credited with inventing Japan's kana syllabary.

entries.name: Kumarila Bhatta
entries.tldr: A 7th-century Mimamsa philosopher who defended the authority of the Vedas against Buddhist challenges. He developed 'intrinsic validity' — cognitions are self-certifying unless specifically challenged. He debated Dharmakirti and is associated with Shankara's teacher.

entries.name: Kundakunda
entries.tldr: A 2nd-century Jain mystic philosopher whose Samayasara is the most important text in the Digambara Jain tradition. He distinguished the phenomenal and transcendental standpoints — from the transcendental standpoint, the pure soul is already free, and practice is about recognizing this.

entries.name: Lacan, Jacques
entries.desc: Psychoanalysis; the unconscious structured like language.
entries.tldr: A French psychoanalyst-philosopher who re-read Freud through structuralist linguistics. 'The unconscious is structured like a language.' The Real, Symbolic, and Imaginary are the three orders of experience. His concepts of the objet petit a and the phallus as signifier influenced continental philosophy and film theory.

entries.name: Langer, Susanne
entries.desc: Philosophy of art; symbol, feeling, form.
entries.tldr: An American philosopher of mind and aesthetics who argued that art is a mode of knowledge — a form of symbolic expression that gives cognitive access to the life of feeling. Her Philosophy in a New Key influenced aesthetics, music theory, and philosophy of mind.

entries.name: Laozi
entries.tldr: The legendary author of the Daodejing — 81 short, paradoxical chapters that form the foundation of Daoism. The Dao cannot be named or defined; it is the silent ground of all transformations. True wisdom consists in wu wei — non-action or effortless action. Whether Laozi was a historical person is disputed.

entries.name: Leibniz, Gottfried Wilhelm
entries.tldr: A German polymath who independently co-invented calculus. His metaphysics holds that reality consists entirely of 'monads' — soul-like, windowless, non-spatial substances that mirror the universe from their own perspective. God chose this as the best of all possible worlds. This optimism was satirized by Voltaire as 'Pangloss' in Candide.

entries.name: Levinas, Emmanuel
entries.desc: Ethics of the Other; face-to-face responsibility.
entries.tldr: A Lithuanian-French philosopher who argued that ethics — not ontology — is first philosophy. The encounter with another person's face is an absolute demand: 'Do not kill me.' This infinite responsibility to the Other precedes consciousness. He survived the Holocaust in a German POW camp; most of his family in Lithuania did not.

entries.name: Lewis, David
entries.tldr: The most systematic analytic metaphysician of the 20th century. His modal realism holds that all possible worlds are just as real as the actual world. This grounded his analysis of counterfactuals, causation, laws of nature, and mental content. Many find the view incredible; almost all agree it is the most internally consistent position available.

entries.name: Locke, John
entries.desc: Empiricism; tabula rasa, social contract, toleration.
entries.tldr: The father of liberalism. Locke argued knowledge derives from experience, that the mind begins as a 'tabula rasa,' and that people have natural rights to life, liberty, and property. His Letter Concerning Toleration argued for religious pluralism. His Two Treatises justified revolution against tyrannical government. He influenced the American founding documents directly.

entries.name: Lu Xiangshan
entries.desc: Neo-Confucian; founder of School of Mind.
entries.tldr: A 12th-century Song dynasty Neo-Confucian who founded the School of Mind, arguing 'the universe is my mind and my mind is the universe.' Against Zhu Xi's insistence on investigating things, Lu held that moral principle is immediately present in the mind — self-examination is all that is needed.

entries.name: Lucretius
entries.desc: Epicurean poet; De Rerum Natura, atomism.
entries.tldr: A 1st-century BCE Roman poet whose De Rerum Natura is the fullest surviving account of Epicurean atomism. Everything — including the soul — consists of atoms. Death is nothing to fear because you will simply cease to perceive. His argument for a random 'swerve' in atomic motion grounds free will in a materialist universe.

entries.name: Lukács, György
entries.tldr: A Hungarian Marxist whose History and Class Consciousness (1923) introduced 'reification' to Western Marxism — the process by which social relations take on the appearance of natural facts. His concept of 'imputed' class consciousness became central to Western Marxism.

entries.name: Lyotard, Jean-François
entries.tldr: The philosopher who named the postmodern condition. The Postmodern Condition (1979) argues the grand metanarratives that legitimated modernity — progress, emancipation, science as the truth — have lost credibility. His concept of the 'differend' analyzes injustice as the silencing of one phrase-regime by another.

entries.name: Macaulay, Catharine
entries.desc: Republican political theory; history, women's education.
entries.tldr: An 18th-century English republican historian and philosopher who argued that women's apparent inferiority was entirely due to education. Mary Wollstonecraft was directly influenced by her. Her 8-volume History of England was a Whig republican classic.

entries.name: MacIntyre, Alasdair
entries.tldr: A Scottish moral philosopher who argued in After Virtue (1981) that modern moral philosophy is incoherent because it uses fragments of older traditions stripped of the narrative contexts that gave them meaning. He advocates a return to Aristotelian teleology embedded in narrative traditions.

entries.name: Mackie, J. L.
entries.desc: Moral error theory; argument from queerness.
entries.tldr: An Australian philosopher known for moral error theory — moral statements are genuine assertions, but there are no objective moral facts, so morality is false. His 'argument from queerness' holds that if objective moral properties existed, they would be unlike anything else in the universe.

entries.name: Madhva
entries.tldr: The 13th-century founder of Dvaita (dualist) Vedanta — the most thoroughgoing dualism in Indian philosophy. Madhva argued that the soul, world, and God are eternally distinct. His doctrine of eternal damnation for some souls is unique in Indian philosophy.

entries.name: Maimonides
entries.desc: Guide for the Perplexed; reconciled Aristotle and Judaism.
entries.tldr: The greatest Jewish philosopher and one of the great philosophers of the medieval world. His Guide for the Perplexed reconciles Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish scripture through allegorical interpretation. Negative theology: we cannot say what God is, only what God is not. Influenced Aquinas, Spinoza, and medieval Christian and Islamic theology.

entries.name: Malebranche, Nicolas
entries.tldr: A French Oratorian who combined Descartes with Augustine. His occasionalism argued God is the only real cause — apparent causal connections between events are just occasions for God to act. We 'see all things in God' — ideas are in the divine intellect, not the human.

entries.name: Mandana Misra
entries.tldr: An 8th-century philosopher who was one of the greatest figures of both Mimamsa and Advaita. He debated Shankara (reportedly losing). His Brahmasiddhi defends Advaita and his work on the philosophy of language — especially sphota theory — is among the most sophisticated in the tradition.

entries.name: Marcuse, Herbert
entries.tldr: A Frankfurt School philosopher whose One-Dimensional Man (1964) argued that advanced industrial society has eliminated genuine opposition through consumerism. He became a hero of the student movements of '68. Eros and Civilization argued that the surplus repression imposed by civilization is not necessary.

entries.name: Mariátegui, José Carlos
entries.desc: Marxist analysis of Peruvian and indigenous reality.
entries.tldr: A Peruvian Marxist who was the most original Latin American thinker of his generation. His Seven Interpretive Essays on Peruvian Reality argued that socialist revolution must incorporate indigenous Andean communal traditions rather than importing a European model.

entries.name: Marx, Karl
entries.tldr: The most influential political thinker of the 19th and 20th centuries. Marx argued that human history is driven by class struggle rooted in the mode of production. Capitalism creates alienation — workers separated from their labor, from other humans, from their own potential. His prediction of communist revolution was partly confirmed and mostly falsified.

entries.name: Masham, Damaris Cudworth
entries.tldr: A 17th-century English philosopher, daughter of Ralph Cudworth, and close friend of John Locke (who died in her home). She argued against occasionalism and wrote on reason in religion and women's intellectual engagement with Christian life.

entries.name: Matilal, Bimal Krishna
entries.desc: Analytic interpreter of Nyaya logic and epistemology.
entries.tldr: An Indian philosopher who spent much of his career at Oxford and became the leading bridge between Western analytic philosophy and classical Indian logic. His works on Nyaya showed that classical Indian logic was as rigorous as anything in the Western tradition.

entries.name: Mbembe, Achille
entries.desc: Postcolony, necropolitics; critique of black reason.
entries.tldr: A Cameroonian philosopher based in South Africa. His concept of 'necropolitics' — the power to determine who may live and who must die — extends Foucault's 'biopolitics.' His Critique of Black Reason examines the history of race as a global system.

entries.name: McDowell, John
entries.tldr: A Pittsburgh philosopher who argues against the 'myth of the given' — that experience can justify belief without itself being a conceptual achievement. Perception is already suffused with conceptual content. His Mind and World argues that the natural and the rational are not opposed.

entries.name: Mead, George Herbert
entries.tldr: An American pragmatist social philosopher who developed the theory of the 'social self.' Selfhood arises through taking the attitude of others toward oneself — the 'generalized other' is internalized. Consciousness and language are inherently social. His Mind, Self, and Society became a founding text of symbolic interactionism.

entries.name: Mencius
entries.desc: Confucian; innate goodness of human nature.
entries.tldr: The 'Second Sage' of Confucianism, Mencius argued humans have four 'sprouts' — the beginnings of humaneness, righteousness, ritual propriety, and wisdom — that need cultivation to flourish. A ruler who nourishes people is legitimate; one who fails to may be overthrown.

entries.name: Merleau-Ponty, Maurice
