entries.tldr: A French phenomenologist who made the body central to philosophy. Against Cartesian mind-body dualism, he argued that perception and cognition are rooted in bodily being-in-the-world. The 'motor intentionality' of skilled action is a form of knowledge that is not representational.

entries.name: Midgley, Mary
entries.desc: Moral philosophy; critique of scientism, human nature.
entries.tldr: A British moral philosopher who criticized reductive scientism and argued for the irreducibility of moral concepts. She began publishing major books at 59 and continued into her 90s. Her Beast and Man examined the relationship between human nature and animal nature.

entries.name: Mill, Harriet Taylor
entries.desc: Women's rights and liberty; collaborator with J.S. Mill.
entries.tldr: John Stuart Mill's intellectual partner, credited with writing The Enfranchisement of Women (1851) arguing for full political equality for women. Mill claimed she was the primary moral and intellectual force in their intellectual partnership.

entries.name: Mill, John Stuart
entries.tldr: The most important liberal political philosopher of the 19th century. Mill's harm principle — the only justification for coercion is to prevent harm to others — remains the cornerstone of liberal political theory. His utilitarianism modified Bentham by distinguishing the quality as well as quantity of pleasures.

entries.name: Mohanty, J. N.
entries.desc: Phenomenology bridging Husserl and Indian philosophy.
entries.tldr: An Indian-American phenomenologist who devoted his career to showing that Husserlian phenomenology and classical Indian philosophy are engaged with the same deep problems. A major figure in both traditions.

entries.name: Montaigne, Michel de
entries.tldr: The inventor of the essay as a philosophical form. His Essays are explorations of himself as a specimen of humanity — changing, contradictory, uncertain. His skepticism was moderate and practical: 'What do I know?' He advocated tolerance in the face of religious wars and compassion for indigenous peoples over European colonizers.

entries.name: Montesquieu
entries.desc: Separation of powers; spirit of the laws.
entries.tldr: An 18th-century French philosopher whose Spirit of the Laws analyzed the relationship between political institutions and social, geographic, and climatic conditions. He developed the theory of separation of powers. His influence on the American and French constitutions was direct.

entries.name: Moore, G. E.
entries.desc: Common sense, open question argument; founder of analytic.
entries.tldr: One of the founders of analytic philosophy. His 'open question argument': no matter what natural property you identify as 'good,' it always makes sense to ask 'but is it really good?' So goodness is not a natural property — naming the 'naturalistic fallacy.'

entries.name: Mozi
entries.desc: Founder of Mohism; impartial care, consequentialism.
entries.tldr: The founder of Mohism, which rivaled Confucianism before being eclipsed. Mozi advocated 'universal care' — equal concern for all people regardless of their relation to you — against Confucian priority given to family. He developed early logic and organized disciples into a kind of philosophical militia.

entries.name: Mudimbe, V. Y.
entries.desc: The Invention of Africa; coloniality of knowledge.
entries.tldr: A Congolese-American philosopher whose The Invention of Africa (1988) argued that Africa as a concept is a Western colonial invention — the product of a 'colonial library' that constitutes African subjects as primitive and in need of Western development.

entries.name: Mulla Sadra
entries.tldr: The greatest figure in later Islamic philosophy. His key innovation: existence (wujud) is the fundamental reality, not essence. Existence has degrees of intensity, and mystical experience is the ascent to more intense being. He synthesized Avicennan philosophy, Sufi metaphysics, and Quranic theology.

entries.name: Murdoch, Iris
entries.desc: Moral vision; the good, attention, Platonism.
entries.tldr: A British novelist and philosopher who argued against existentialism's obsession with will and choice. The moral life is primarily about attention — seeing reality clearly, especially other people. The Good is real and demanding. Her The Sovereignty of Good is one of the finest short works in moral philosophy.

entries.name: Nagarjuna
entries.tldr: The founder of Madhyamaka ('middle way') Buddhism and one of the greatest philosophers in any tradition. His Mulamadhyamakakarika argues that all phenomena are empty of inherent existence — they arise through dependent origination. But emptiness itself is also empty — there is no ultimate foundation. This is not nihilism: conventional reality remains valid.

entries.name: Nagel, Thomas
entries.desc: “What is it like to be a bat?”; subjectivity, ethics.
entries.tldr: An American philosopher famous for 'What is it like to be a bat?' — the argument that physicalist theories cannot capture the subjective character of experience. His Mind and Cosmos argues that materialism cannot account for consciousness, cognition, and value without extending our concept of nature.

entries.name: Nicholas of Cusa
entries.tldr: A 15th-century German cardinal who developed a mystical-philosophical vision of God as the 'coincidence of opposites.' His Learned Ignorance argues that human knowledge can only approach God asymptotically. He was the first to suggest the universe might be infinite and without a center, anticipating Copernicus.

entries.name: Nietzsche, Friedrich
entries.desc: Will to power, genealogy of morals, eternal recurrence.
entries.tldr: The most provocative philosopher of the 19th century. He argued that 'God is dead' — requiring a revaluation of all values. The 'will to power' is self-overcoming. Eternal recurrence — would you live your life again, exactly as it has been, infinitely? — is a test of life-affirmation. The Übermensch creates new values.

entries.name: Nimbarka
entries.tldr: A 12th-century Telugu philosopher who founded the dvaitadvaita ('difference and non-difference') school of Vedanta. He argued that the relationship between soul, world, and Brahman is one of simultaneous difference and identity — neither Shankara's pure non-dualism nor Madhva's pure dualism.

entries.name: Nishida Kitaro
entries.desc: Founder of Kyoto School; pure experience, nothingness.
entries.tldr: The founder of the Kyoto School and Japan's first major original philosopher. He developed the concept of 'pure experience' — immediate experience prior to the subject-object split — as the ground of all reality. His later concept of 'absolute nothingness' aims to articulate East Asian experience in Western philosophical terms.

entries.name: Nozick, Robert
entries.tldr: A Harvard libertarian philosopher whose Anarchy, State, and Utopia (1974) was a direct response to Rawls. Against Rawlsian egalitarianism, any more than a minimal 'nightwatchman' state violates rights. His 'experience machine' asks whether you would plug into a machine providing any experience you wanted — to show that what matters is actually doing things.

entries.name: Nussbaum, Martha
entries.tldr: A Harvard/Chicago philosopher known for the 'capabilities approach' — justice requires securing a list of central human capabilities for all people. Her Upheavals of Thought argues that emotions are intelligent appraisals of value and indispensable to ethical life.

entries.name: O'Neill, Onora
entries.tldr: A British Kantian philosopher who has applied Kantian ethics to practical questions with unusual clarity. Her work on trust distinguishes trustworthiness from mere predictability. Her work on bioethics and global justice applies constructivist Kantian principles to real-world questions.

entries.name: Ockham, William of
entries.tldr: The 'Venerable Inceptor' and the sharpest mind of the late medieval period. His razor: 'entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity.' He was a nominalist: only individuals exist; universals are just names. He fled to the Emperor after a conflict with the Pope over Franciscan poverty.

entries.name: Ogyu Sorai
entries.desc: Ancient Learning Confucian; Way of the sage-kings.
entries.tldr: An 18th-century Japanese Confucian who rejected Neo-Confucian metaphysics and called for a return to the ancient Kings of China as the source of the Way. For Sorai, the Way is a set of concrete institutions and rites established by sage-kings, not a moral or cosmological reality.

entries.name: Oruka, Henry Odera
entries.tldr: A Kenyan philosopher who developed 'sage philosophy' — the idea that traditional African sages who engage in critical, reflective discourse are doing genuine philosophy. Against ethnophilosophy (which collectivizes African thought) and against the view that Africa has no philosophy, he identified individual African sages engaged in rigorous argument. His project involved fieldwork interviewing Kenyan sages directly.

entries.name: Parfit, Derek
entries.desc: Personal identity, reasons; Reasons and Persons.
entries.tldr: A British philosopher whose Reasons and Persons (1984) is one of the most important works in 20th-century ethics and metaphysics. He argued personal identity is not what matters — 'what matters' is psychological continuity. His population ethics produced the 'repugnant conclusion.' He believed ethics showed that morality is objective.

entries.name: Parmenides
entries.desc: Being is one and unchanging; founder of metaphysics.
entries.tldr: The founder of Western metaphysics and one of the most radical thinkers in any tradition. His poem argues that 'being is' and 'non-being is not' — change, plurality, and motion are therefore illusory. True reality is one, whole, motionless, eternal, and unchanging. Plato wrote a dialogue in which Parmenides destroys the Theory of Forms.

entries.name: Pascal, Blaise
entries.tldr: A French mathematician who invented the first mechanical calculator, then renounced science after a mystical experience. His Pensées contains the wager argument: if God exists and you believe, infinite gain; if not, finite loss. So rationality requires betting on God.

entries.name: Patanjali
entries.tldr: The compiler of the Yoga Sutras, the foundational text of classical Yoga philosophy. He describes Yoga as 'the cessation of the fluctuations of mind-stuff' and lays out an eightfold path from ethical restraints through postures, breathing, and concentration to absorption (samadhi).

entries.name: Peirce, Charles Sanders
entries.tldr: The founder of American pragmatism. Truth is what inquiry converges on in the long run; meaning is identified with experiential difference. He invented the logic of relations and made foundational contributions to probability theory. His semiotic — the theory of signs — is the most systematic ever developed. He died in poverty.

entries.name: Pettit, Philip
entries.desc: Republican political theory; freedom as non-domination.
entries.tldr: An Irish philosopher known for neo-republican political theory — liberty as non-domination rather than mere non-interference. A good master still dominates a slave. Republican freedom requires institutions that make domination impossible, not just absent.

entries.name: Plantinga, Alvin
entries.tldr: A reformed epistemologist who argues that belief in God can be 'properly basic' — rational without being inferred from evidence. His modal version of the ontological argument is the most technically sophisticated in the literature. His evolutionary argument against naturalism argues evolution gives us no reason to trust our cognitive faculties.

entries.name: Plato
entries.desc: Theory of Forms; foundational to Western philosophy.
entries.tldr: The most important philosopher in Western history (with Aristotle). Plato's Theory of Forms holds that the world we perceive is a shadow of a higher reality of eternal, perfect Forms. The Republic describes a state ruled by philosopher-kings trained in dialectic. Almost every subsequent Western philosopher is responding to Plato.

entries.name: Plotinus
entries.desc: Founder of Neoplatonism; the One, emanation.
entries.tldr: The founder of Neoplatonism. Reality emanates from 'the One' — an absolute beyond being and thought — through Nous (intellect) to Soul to Matter. The goal of philosophy is the soul's return to union with the One. His Enneads shaped Christian, Jewish, Islamic, and later Western philosophy.

entries.name: Popper, Karl
entries.tldr: The most influential philosopher of science of the 20th century. Popper's falsificationism: science progresses by attempting to falsify theories — a theory that cannot be falsified is not scientific. Psychoanalysis and Marxism fail this test. His Open Society attacked Plato, Hegel, and Marx as laying the groundwork for totalitarianism.

entries.name: Porphyry
entries.tldr: A Syrian Neoplatonist who edited the Enneads and wrote the Isagoge — which became the standard introduction to logic in the medieval world. His question about universals — are they real, mental, or just names? — launched a thousand years of medieval debate.

entries.name: Prabhakara
entries.desc: Mimamsa; founder of the Prabhakara sub-school.
entries.tldr: A 7th-century Mimamsa philosopher who founded a distinct sub-school. He developed an account of sentence meaning and a distinctive theory of the epistemic situation. His ethical view holds that duty is known directly from the Vedic injunction, not derived from consequences.

entries.name: Proclus
entries.tldr: The most systematic of the late Neoplatonists, whose Elements of Theology gives a rigorous axiomatic derivation of the Neoplatonic hierarchy from 'the One.' He influenced Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (who secretly plagiarized him), and through him, Christian mystical theology including Eckhart and Nicholas of Cusa.

entries.name: Protagoras
entries.desc: Sophist; “man is the measure of all things.”
entries.tldr: The greatest of the Sophists. 'Man is the measure of all things: of things that are, that they are; of things that are not, that they are not.' Truth and falsehood are relative to the perceiver. He claimed to make 'the weaker argument appear stronger.' Plato's particular target in the Protagoras and Theaetetus.

entries.name: Putnam, Hilary
entries.tldr: An American philosopher who made major contributions to philosophy of mind, mathematics, and language. His 'Twin Earth' thought experiment established semantic externalism — meaning 'ain't in the head.' His functionalism was one of the dominant theories of mind for two decades before he himself criticized it.

entries.name: Pyrrho
entries.desc: Founder of Skepticism; suspension of judgment.
entries.tldr: The founder of ancient skepticism, who reportedly lived without holding any beliefs about how things are. He sought ataraxia through suspension of judgment (epoché). We cannot know whether anything is good or bad, true or false, so we should neither affirm nor deny.

entries.name: Pythagoras
entries.desc: Number as reality; transmigration of souls.
entries.tldr: A semi-legendary figure who discovered the mathematical relationships underlying musical harmony and is credited with the theorem bearing his name. He believed the soul was immortal and underwent transmigration, and that numbers are the underlying reality of all things. The Pythagoreans refused to eat beans.

entries.name: Quine, W. V. O.
entries.desc: Two dogmas of empiricism; ontological relativity.
entries.tldr: The most important American philosopher of the mid-20th century. His 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' attacked the analytic-synthetic distinction. Quine's holism: statements face experience as a corporate body — you can revise any statement, including logical laws, in response to recalcitrant experience.

entries.name: Radhakrishnan, Sarvepalli
entries.tldr: India's philosopher-statesman who became independent India's first Vice-President. He argued that Vedanta — particularly Advaita — is capable of encompassing all religious traditions. He interpreted Indian philosophy for Western audiences and Western philosophy for Indians, creating a synthesis that remains controversial in both directions.

entries.name: Raghunatha Siromani
entries.tldr: The greatest Navya-Nyaya logician of the 15th–16th centuries who developed the technical vocabulary of the school to unprecedented precision. The 'Navadvipa school' he founded dominated Indian logic for two centuries.

entries.name: Ramanuja
entries.tldr: The 12th-century founder of Vishishtadvaita — 'qualified non-dualism.' Against Shankara, he argued that Brahman is not featureless but is personal — Vishnu himself. Individual souls and matter are real but exist as attributes of Brahman. Devotion (bhakti) to Vishnu is the path to liberation.

entries.name: Ramsey, Frank
entries.tldr: A Cambridge philosopher who died at 26 but left papers that anticipated major advances in logic, probability theory, and economics. His decision-theoretic interpretation of utility and probability was decades ahead of its time. Russell thought he had the best philosophical mind of his generation.

entries.name: Rand, Ayn
entries.tldr: A Russian-American novelist and philosopher who developed Objectivism — rational egoism, and laissez-faire capitalism as the only just political system. Her novels Atlas Shrugged and The Fountainhead dramatize these ideas. Enormously influential in libertarian and conservative circles; largely dismissed by academic philosophers.

entries.name: Rawls, John
entries.desc: A Theory of Justice; justice as fairness.
entries.tldr: The most important political philosopher of the 20th century. A Theory of Justice (1971) revived the social contract tradition with the 'original position' — imagine choosing principles of justice from behind a 'veil of ignorance.' Result: equal basic liberties for all, inequalities only if they benefit the least advantaged.

entries.name: Raz, Joseph
entries.tldr: A British legal philosopher who developed the 'service conception of authority.' His perfectionist liberalism holds that the state may promote valuable forms of life, contra Rawls's neutrality. The Morality of Freedom is the most sophisticated defense of perfectionism in contemporary political philosophy.

entries.name: Ricoeur, Paul
entries.tldr: A French philosopher who worked across phenomenology, hermeneutics, narrative, and ethics. He argued that the self is constituted through narrative — personal identity is the identity of the character in a story. His three-volume Time and Narrative analyzes the relationship between time-consciousness and narrative form.

entries.name: Rorty, Richard
entries.tldr: The most provocative American philosopher of his generation. Rorty argued that the mirror of nature — the idea that the mind accurately represents the world — is a harmful illusion. Truth is what is good for us to believe. Philosophy should stop trying to be a foundational discipline and become just another voice in cultural conversation.

entries.name: Rousseau, Jean-Jacques
entries.desc: Social contract; general will, natural goodness.
entries.tldr: A Genevan philosopher who profoundly influenced the French Revolution. His Discourse on Inequality argued that private property created inequality and the corruptions of civilization. The Social Contract developed the concept of the 'general will' — the collective interest distinct from the sum of private interests.

entries.name: Russell, Bertrand
entries.desc: Logicism, theory of descriptions; Principia Mathematica.
entries.tldr: One of the founders of analytic philosophy and greatest logicians. His theory of descriptions dissolved apparent puzzles about reference. His Principia Mathematica (with Whitehead) attempted to derive mathematics from logic. He was imprisoned for pacifism in World War I and won the Nobel Prize for Literature.

entries.name: Ryle, Gilbert
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher whose The Concept of Mind (1949) attacked Cartesian dualism as 'the ghost in the machine' — a category mistake. The mind is a set of dispositions to behavior. His distinction between 'knowing-that' and 'knowing-how' has been enormously influential in philosophy of action and education.

entries.name: Saadia Gaon
entries.desc: First systematic Jewish philosopher; Jewish Kalam.
entries.tldr: The first systematic Jewish philosopher, who wrote the first systematic philosophical defense of Jewish theology using Islamic Kalam methods. He argued for creation ex nihilo, against astrology, for free will, and for the compatibility of reason and revelation.

entries.name: Sandel, Michael
entries.tldr: A Harvard political philosopher known for his communitarian critique of Rawlsian liberalism. He argues that Rawls's 'unencumbered self' is incoherent: we are always already members of communities whose goods define us. His Justice: What's the Right Thing to Do? brought philosophy of justice to a mass audience.

entries.name: Sartre, Jean-Paul
entries.tldr: The most famous philosopher of the 20th century, the face of existentialism. 'Existence precedes essence' — we are radically free and entirely responsible for what we make of ourselves. 'Bad faith' is pretending we have no choice. He refused the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1964.

entries.name: Scanlon, T. M.
entries.desc: Contractualism; What We Owe to Each Other.
entries.tldr: A Harvard moral philosopher who develops 'contractualism' — an action is wrong if it violates a principle that no one could reasonably reject. His 'buck-passing' account of value: something is good not because goodness is a property it has but because it has other properties that give us reason to value it.

entries.name: Schelling, F. W. J.
entries.desc: German idealism; philosophy of nature, identity.
entries.tldr: The most Romantic of the German idealists, Schelling argued for a philosophy of nature, a philosophy of identity, and a late 'positive philosophy' that begins from existence rather than essence. He influenced Hegel (who later criticized him), Coleridge, and Heidegger.

entries.name: Schopenhauer, Arthur
entries.desc: World as will and representation; pessimism.
entries.tldr: The great pessimist. Behind Kant's appearances lies the Will — a blind, striving, insatiable cosmic force. This explains suffering: we cannot fulfill the Will and cannot stop wanting. Liberation comes through aesthetic contemplation and ultimately through asceticism. He introduced Indian philosophy to European philosophy.

entries.name: Searle, John
entries.desc: Speech acts; Chinese Room, social ontology.
entries.tldr: A Berkeley philosopher known for the Chinese Room argument against strong AI. A person following rules for Chinese symbols without understanding Chinese parallels a computer — neither understands. He argues for 'biological naturalism' — consciousness is caused by brain processes and cannot be reduced to computation.

entries.name: Sellars, Wilfrid
entries.desc: Myth of the given; scientific image of man.
entries.tldr: A Pittsburgh philosopher whose 'Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind' (1956) attacked 'the myth of the given' — the idea that there are foundational sense-data that justify beliefs without themselves requiring justification. All epistemic relations are within 'the space of reasons,' a normative domain.

entries.name: Sen, Amartya
entries.tldr: An Indian economist-philosopher who developed the capabilities approach to development and justice. Per capita income fails to capture what matters about human flourishing — what matters is what people are able to do and be. His Poverty and Famines showed that famines are caused by failures of entitlement. Nobel Prize in Economics 1998.

entries.name: Seneca
entries.desc: Roman Stoic; ethics, letters on living.
entries.tldr: A Roman Stoic philosopher, playwright, and statesman — Nero's tutor — who wrote elegant essays and letters on how to live. His Letters to Lucilius cover how to face death, how to use time, how to maintain inner freedom under external pressure. He was ordered by Nero to commit suicide and did so with studied Stoic calm.

entries.name: Sextus Empiricus
entries.desc: Pyrrhonist skeptic; chief source on ancient skepticism.
entries.tldr: A physician and Pyrrhonist skeptic who is the main source for our knowledge of ancient skepticism. His Outlines of Pyrrhonism presents arguments against every dogmatic philosophical position — not to establish anything but to induce suspension of judgment and thereby ataraxia.

entries.name: Shankara
entries.tldr: The greatest systematic philosopher in Indian history and the definitive interpreter of Advaita Vedanta. He argued that Brahman alone is real — the world of plurality is maya (ontological misapprehension). The individual self (atman) is ultimately identical to Brahman. His commentaries established the curriculum of Advaita and are still read and debated today.

entries.name: Shantarakshita
entries.desc: Yogacara-Madhyamaka synthesis; brought Buddhism to Tibet.
entries.tldr: An 8th-century Indian philosopher who synthesized Madhyamaka and Yogacara, arguing both are needed. He was instrumental in bringing Buddhism to Tibet, building the first Buddhist monastery at Samye. His Tattvasamgraha is an encyclopedic survey and critique of all Indian philosophical schools.

entries.name: Shantideva
entries.tldr: An 8th-century Madhyamaka monk whose Bodhicaryavatara ('Guide to the Bodhisattva's Way of Life') is the most beloved text in Tibetan Buddhism. He argues for the bodhisattva ideal through rigorous arguments for compassion and against self-grasping. His meditation on the equality of self and other is practiced in Tibetan meditation to this day.

entries.name: Shepherd, Mary
entries.desc: Causation, perception; critique of Hume and Berkeley.
entries.tldr: A Scottish philosopher who was one of the sharpest critics of Hume in her own time. Her Essays upon the Relation of Cause and Effect directly attacked Hume's skepticism about causation, arguing that causation is a necessary connection that can be established by reason.

entries.name: Singer, Peter
entries.desc: Practical ethics; animal liberation, effective altruism.
entries.tldr: The most influential living applied ethicist. His Animal Liberation (1975) argued that speciesism — discriminating against animals merely because they are not human — is no more defensible than racism. He is the main philosophical influence behind the effective altruism movement.

entries.name: Smith, Adam
entries.desc: Moral sentiments; wealth of nations, sympathy.
entries.tldr: The founder of economics is misremembered as the prophet of selfishness. His Theory of Moral Sentiments argues morality is grounded in sympathy — the imaginative capacity to see things from another's point of view. The Wealth of Nations argues individual self-interest channels through markets — but within a framework of justice.

entries.name: Sober, Elliott
entries.tldr: A Wisconsin philosopher of biology who has clarified debates about natural selection, parsimony, and evolution. His Evidence and Evolution is the most rigorous analysis of the logic of evolutionary arguments.

entries.name: Socrates
entries.tldr: The figure who defines what a philosopher is. Socrates wrote nothing. He went around Athens asking what justice, courage, piety, and knowledge are — and showing that everyone who thought they knew, didn't. 'I know that I know nothing.' He drank hemlock rather than renounce his mission.

entries.name: Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz
entries.desc: Colonial Mexican proto-feminist; knowledge and reason.
entries.tldr: A 17th-century Mexican nun and the greatest poet of the Spanish colonial period who also wrote philosophical defenses of women's right to intellectual life. Her Reply to Sor Philothea argues that women have as much right as men to pursue learning.

entries.name: Sosa, Ernest
entries.tldr: An American philosopher who developed virtue epistemology — knowledge is a matter of cognitive achievement, not just lucky true belief. His distinction between 'animal knowledge' and 'reflective knowledge' has been influential.

entries.name: Spinoza, Baruch
entries.tldr: One of the greatest rationalists and one of the most radical philosophers in history. Spinoza's Ethics argues that God and Nature are identical — there is only one substance. There is no free will, no sin, and no personal God who answers prayers. He was excommunicated from his Amsterdam Jewish community at 23. Hegel called him 'the highest point of modern philosophy.'

entries.name: Stalnaker, Robert
entries.tldr: An American philosopher who developed possible worlds semantics for conditional statements and a sophisticated theory of mental content. His belief states are 'self-locating': to believe something is to be disposed to act as if you occupy a certain possible world.

entries.name: Stein, Edith
entries.tldr: A student of Husserl who became a Carmelite nun and was killed at Auschwitz. Her philosophical work analyzes empathy — how we come to understand other minds — phenomenologically. She was canonized by John Paul II in 1998.

entries.name: Strawson, P. F.
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher who developed 'descriptive metaphysics.' His 'Freedom and Resentment' (1962) argues that our practices of holding people responsible are grounded in the 'reactive attitudes' — resentment, gratitude, indignation — that constitute our interpersonal lives, not in metaphysical facts about causation.

entries.name: Suhrawardi
entries.tldr: The founder of Illuminationist (Ishraqi) philosophy in 12th-century Iran. He argued reality is organized as a hierarchy of lights, descending from the Light of Lights (God). Knowledge is direct illumination — the soul can be lit up by a higher light directly. He was executed at 36, reportedly on orders of Saladin.

entries.name: Swinburne, Richard
entries.desc: Natural theology; probabilistic arguments for God.
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher of religion who has made the most sustained contemporary attempt to argue for Christian theism using probabilistic reasoning. His The Existence of God applies Bayesian confirmation theory to the evidence for God.

entries.name: Tagore, Rabindranath
entries.tldr: India's greatest modern poet also wrote essays developing a philosophy of universal humanism rooted in aesthetic experience. His debates with Gandhi about nationalism and with Einstein about science and reality are landmarks in intellectual history. His Gitanjali won the 1913 Nobel Prize for Literature.

entries.name: Tarski, Alfred
entries.desc: Semantic theory of truth; model theory.
entries.tldr: A Polish-American logician who gave the first rigorous definition of truth for formal languages: 'Snow is white' is true if and only if snow is white — the T-schema. This replaced both coherence and correspondence theories with a formal apparatus. He also developed model theory.

entries.name: Taylor, Charles
entries.desc: Communitarianism; sources of the self, secularity.
entries.tldr: A Canadian philosopher known for communitarianism and 'sources of the self.' Against liberal neutrality, he argues that modern selfhood is constituted by 'strong evaluations' embedded in traditions and communities. His A Secular Age is a massive account of how Western societies became secular.

entries.name: Thales
entries.desc: First philosopher; water as first principle.
entries.tldr: The first philosopher in the Western tradition. He proposed that water is the fundamental substance from which all else is derived. He predicted a solar eclipse and reportedly fell into a well while stargazing. Aristotle identified him as the first philosopher.

entries.name: Thomson, Judith Jarvis
entries.desc: Trolley problem; rights, the ethics of abortion.
entries.tldr: A MIT moral philosopher famous for the violinist thought experiment in defense of abortion rights: even if the fetus has full moral status, abortion can be permissible because no one has the right to use your body for nine months without consent. Also developed the trolley problem jointly with Foot.

entries.name: Tsongkhapa
entries.desc: Founder of Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism.
entries.tldr: The 14th-century founder of the Gelug school of Tibetan Buddhism. His Great Treatise on the Stages of the Path is the most comprehensive synthesis of Buddhist practice in Tibetan. He emphasized Prasangika Madhyamaka as the correct view of emptiness and the compatibility of monastic discipline with Tantric practice.

entries.name: Tusi, Nasir al-Din
entries.desc: Polymath; ethics, logic, astronomy, Avicennan revival.
entries.tldr: A 13th-century Persian polymath who worked for the Mongol Khan, building an observatory at Maragha. His Nasirean Ethics is a standard work. He made important corrections to Ptolemaic astronomy that anticipated Copernicus and was a leading philosopher in the Avicennan tradition.

entries.name: Udayana
entries.tldr: A 10th-century Nyaya philosopher who wrote the Nyayakusumanjali — a systematic proof of God's existence using Nyaya categories. His arguments were the primary target of Buddhist anti-theist philosophers and remain philosophically interesting.

entries.name: Uddyotakara
entries.desc: Naiyayika; defended Nyaya against Buddhist logic.
entries.tldr: A 6th-7th century Naiyayika who wrote the Nyayavarttika in defense of Nyaya against Dignaga's Buddhist criticisms. His debates with Dignaga represent one of the most technically demanding exchanges in classical Indian philosophy.

entries.name: Umasvati
entries.desc: Jain; Tattvartha Sutra, authoritative across sects.
entries.tldr: The author of the Tattvartha Sutra — 'That which is' — the most authoritative systematic text in Jain philosophy, accepted by both major Jain sects. Its doctrine of seven categories and the conditions for liberation through non-attachment define classical Jain philosophy.

entries.name: Unamuno, Miguel de
entries.desc: Tragic sense of life; faith, existential reason.
entries.tldr: A Spanish existentialist avant la lettre who put the 'tragic sense of life' — the desire for immortality in conflict with the certainty of death — at the center of his philosophy. His The Tragic Sense of Life argues philosophy should proceed from the existential situation of real human beings.

entries.name: Vacaspati Misra
entries.desc: Commentator on all six orthodox systems; Bhamati Advaita.
entries.tldr: A 9th-10th century philosopher who wrote authoritative subcommentaries on all six orthodox systems of Indian philosophy. This encyclopedic achievement is unique in the history of philosophy. His Bhamati commentary on Shankara founded a distinct Advaita sub-school.

entries.name: Vallabha
entries.tldr: A 15th-16th century Telugu philosopher who founded Shuddhadvaita ('pure non-dualism') Vedanta. He argued Brahman (Krishna) creates the world out of himself without any limiting adjuncts — the world is a real transformation of Brahman. Liberation is pushtimarga — the 'path of grace,' flowing from Krishna's freely given love.

entries.name: van Fraassen, Bas
entries.tldr: A Canadian philosopher of science who developed 'constructive empiricism' — science does not aim at truth but at empirical adequacy. We should believe only that theories are empirically adequate, not that theoretical entities like electrons really exist.

entries.name: van Inwagen, Peter
entries.tldr: An American philosopher known for the 'consequence argument' for hard incompatibilism about free will: if determinism is true, our actions are consequences of laws before our birth — we could not have done otherwise. He also holds that only simples and organisms exist — chairs and tables are not really there.

entries.name: Vasconcelos, José
entries.desc: The Cosmic Race; Mexican philosophy of identity.
entries.tldr: The Mexican philosopher who wrote The Cosmic Race (1925), arguing that Latin America — with its mixture of European, indigenous, African, and Asian strains — would produce a new universal 'cosmic race' that synthesizes humanity. A utopian counter-narrative to Anglo-American racial hierarchy.

entries.name: Vasubandhu
entries.tldr: The most prolific philosopher in the Buddhist tradition. His Abhidharmakosa is the definitive account of Abhidharma Buddhist metaphysics. His Vimsatika is the most rigorous argument for consciousness-only idealism in Indian philosophy.

entries.name: Vatsyayana
entries.desc: Author of Nyaya Bhasya, foundational Nyaya commentary.
entries.tldr: The 5th-century author of the Nyayabhasya — the foundational commentary on the Nyaya Sutras — establishing the basic Nyaya positions on perception, inference, and the soul, on which all subsequent Naiyayikas commented.

entries.name: Vivekananda, Swami
entries.desc: Neo-Vedanta; introduced Hindu philosophy to the West.
entries.tldr: The disciple of Ramakrishna who introduced Advaita Vedanta and yoga to the West at the 1893 Parliament of World's Religions in Chicago. His Neo-Vedanta interpreted Advaita as a universal religion compatible with science. One of the most important figures in modern Hinduism.

entries.name: Voltaire
entries.tldr: France's most famous Enlightenment writer and philosopher who wielded wit against religious intolerance and political despotism. His Candide savaged Leibniz's optimism. He campaigned for victims of religious persecution with remarkable effectiveness.

entries.name: Wang Bi
entries.desc: Xuanxue; commentator on Daodejing and Yijing.
entries.tldr: A 3rd-century Chinese philosopher and one of the founders of Xuanxue ('Profound Learning') who reinterpreted Daoism. His commentaries interpreted the Dao as 'non-being' — the nameless ground from which all being emerges. He died at 23 but his influence on Chinese philosophy was enormous.

entries.name: Wang Fuzhi
entries.desc: Late Ming materialist; priority of qi, historical change.
entries.tldr: A 17th-century Chinese philosopher who argued that qi (material force) is primary and li (principle) is just its pattern. He wrote from hiding after the Manchu conquest of China, refusing to serve the new dynasty. His philosophy of historical change held that institutions must evolve with the times.

entries.name: Wang Yangming
entries.desc: School of Mind; unity of knowledge and action.
entries.tldr: A 15th-16th century Neo-Confucian who revived the School of Mind. His doctrine of the 'unity of knowledge and action' holds that genuine knowledge of the good necessarily includes acting on it — if you know it and don't act on it, you don't really know it.

entries.name: Warnock, Mary
entries.desc: Applied ethics, philosophy of education, bioethics.
entries.tldr: A British moral philosopher who chaired the Warnock Commission on human fertilization and embryology (1982-84), whose report shaped British legislation on IVF and embryo research. Her philosophical work developed a Kantian approach to practical ethics.

entries.name: Watsuji Tetsuro
entries.desc: Ethics as betweenness; climate and culture.
entries.tldr: A 20th-century Japanese philosopher who developed an ethics of 'betweenness' (aidagara) — the space between persons — against the Western overemphasis on the individual. His Climate and Culture argued human existence is shaped by its geographical environment, creating distinct cultural forms.

entries.name: Weil, Simone
entries.tldr: A French philosopher, mystic, and political activist who is among the most extraordinary thinkers of the 20th century. 'Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity.' She starved herself to death in solidarity with occupied France. Her notebooks were published posthumously as Gravity and Grace.

entries.name: West, Cornel
entries.tldr: An African American philosopher who developed 'prophetic pragmatism' — a synthesis of American pragmatism with Black liberation theology. His Race Matters (1993) brought these arguments to a popular audience. A persistent critic of American racism and Black intellectual establishment complicity.

entries.name: Whitehead, Alfred North
entries.tldr: Bertrand Russell's co-author for Principia Mathematica who turned, in later life, to develop 'process philosophy.' Reality consists not of substances but of events or 'occasions of experience.' His Adventures of Ideas is one of the most ambitious philosophical visions of the 20th century.

entries.name: Williams, Bernard
entries.desc: Critique of utilitarianism; moral luck, integrity.
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher who was the most devastating critic of utilitarianism. His argument from integrity: utilitarianism alienates you from your own commitments by demanding constant impartial calculation. His concept of 'moral luck' showed that our attributions of moral responsibility are more contingent than we like to admit.

entries.name: Williamson, Timothy
entries.tldr: An Oxford philosopher who argues for 'knowledge-first' epistemology — knowledge is the fundamental mental state, not a conjunction of belief, truth, and justification. His book Knowledge and Its Limits is the most sustained attack on the traditional analysis.

entries.name: Wiredu, Kwasi
entries.tldr: A Ghanaian philosopher who argued for 'conceptual decolonization' — African philosophy should rid itself of inherited colonial frameworks and recover its own conceptual resources. His concept of democracy as consensus-seeking, rather than majority-rule voting, draws on Akan political practice.

entries.name: Wittgenstein, Ludwig
entries.tldr: Perhaps the most original philosopher of the 20th century — arguably writing two entirely different philosophies. The early Tractatus holds that meaningful propositions picture facts. The later Philosophical Investigations argues meaning is use — embedded in 'forms of life.' 'A philosophical problem has the form: I don't know my way about.' He abandoned philosophy twice, both times returning.

entries.name: Wollstonecraft, Mary
entries.desc: Vindication of the Rights of Woman; early feminism.
entries.tldr: The founder of modern feminist political philosophy. A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) argued that women's apparent irrationality is entirely a product of education, not nature. If educated the same as men, women would show the same rational capacities. Mother of Mary Shelley (author of Frankenstein).

entries.name: Wonhyo
entries.desc: Major Korean Buddhist; harmonized Mahayana doctrines.
entries.tldr: The most important Korean Buddhist philosopher, who harmonized the apparently contradictory doctrines of Mahayana Buddhism through the concept of 'one mind.' He left monastic life, married, and wrote prolifically in order to bring Buddhist teaching to ordinary people.

entries.name: Xenophanes
entries.desc: Critique of anthropomorphic gods; early monotheism.
entries.tldr: A 6th-century BCE Greek poet-philosopher who criticized anthropomorphic religion: if horses had gods, they would look like horses. He pioneered theological criticism that influenced all subsequent natural theology. He also noted fossils in rock — evidence that the sea once covered the land.

entries.name: Xunzi
entries.desc: Confucian; human nature needs correction, ritual.
entries.tldr: A 3rd-century BCE Confucian who broke with Mencius by arguing human nature is bad — we are naturally selfish and require ritual, education, and law to become good. His logic of classification and theory of ritual as social technology make him one of the most philosophically rigorous Confucians.

entries.name: Yashovijaya
entries.desc: Jain monk; applied Navya-Nyaya logic to Jain philosophy.
entries.tldr: A 17th-century Jain monk who mastered Navya-Nyaya and applied its technical tools to Jain philosophy. One of the last great synthesis thinkers of the classical Indian tradition.

entries.name: Yi Hwang (Toegye)
entries.desc: Korean Neo-Confucian; li-qi dualism, Four-Seven debate.
entries.tldr: The most important Korean Confucian philosopher, known as 'Korea's Zhu Xi.' His 'Four-Seven debate' on the relationship between the four sprouts and the seven emotions became the central question of Korean Neo-Confucian philosophy. His influence on Korean culture is comparable to Zhu Xi's in China.

entries.name: Yi I (Yulgok)
entries.desc: Korean Neo-Confucian; emphasized qi, counterpart to Toegye.
entries.tldr: The second great figure of Korean Neo-Confucianism after Toegye. Where Toegye emphasized the separation of li and qi, Yulgok argued for their inseparability. He also wrote practical reform memorials to the Korean king arguing for policy based on Confucian moral principles.

entries.name: Zea, Leopoldo
entries.desc: Philosophy of Latin American identity and history.
entries.tldr: A Mexican philosopher who argued that Latin American philosophy should take Latin American historical experience — colonialism, independence, underdevelopment — as its proper subject matter. One of the founders of 'Latin American philosophy' as a distinct academic field.

entries.name: Zeno of Citium
entries.tldr: The founder of Stoicism, a philosophy that became the dominant ethical framework of the Greek and Roman world for six centuries. The Stoics held that virtue is the only good and that living according to nature and reason — accepting fate, mastering passions — is the highest life.

entries.name: Zeno of Elea
entries.tldr: Parmenides's student and defender who invented a set of paradoxes to defend his master's monism. Achilles can never catch the tortoise; an arrow in flight is at rest at each instant; you cannot cross a stadium. These paradoxes challenged Greek mathematics and philosophy and generated productive response for two millennia.

entries.name: Zhang Zai
entries.desc: Neo-Confucian; philosophy of qi, Western Inscription.
entries.tldr: A Song dynasty Neo-Confucian philosopher whose 'Western Inscription' declares that heaven and earth are our parents and all people our siblings. He developed a philosophy of qi as the fundamental reality, whose transformations produce all things including the mind.

entries.name: Zhou Dunyi
entries.tldr: The pioneer of Neo-Confucian metaphysics whose Taijitu shuo ('Explanation of the Diagram of the Supreme Ultimate') traced the generation of the world from the Supreme Ultimate. He synthesized Daoist cosmology with Confucian ethics, laying the groundwork for the great Song dynasty syntheses.

entries.name: Zhu Xi
entries.desc: Synthesizer of Neo-Confucian School of Principle.
entries.tldr: The great synthesizer of Neo-Confucianism and the most influential Chinese philosopher after Confucius. Zhu Xi argued every thing has its li (principle) and is made of qi (material force). His commentary on the Four Books became the official curriculum for the Chinese civil service examination for 600 years.

entries.name: Zhuangzi
entries.tldr: The second great Daoist thinker after Laozi, Zhuangzi's book is the most delightful work of Chinese philosophy — filled with stories and thought experiments. The cook who butchers an ox following its natural structure; the butterfly who dreams he is a man. Perspectivism: reality looks different from every vantage point, so why privilege any?

entries.name: Žižek, Slavoj
entries.tldr: A Slovenian philosopher who uses Lacanian psychoanalysis and Hegelian dialectics to analyze ideology. His central claim: ideology is not false consciousness that obscures reality; it is the fantasy structure that enables us to act in reality. His The Sublime Object of Ideology (1989) launched his career.
